docs.title: Course Description for Engineering Practicum IV Honors
Course Description: Engineering Practicum IV Honors
Course Overview
docs.text:
Engineering Practicum IV Honors enables students to examine technology and engineering fundamentals related to solving real-world problems. Students examine ethics and intellectual property and design a practicum project — a culmination of knowledge and skill gained in previous engineering courses. In addition, students continue to investigate engineering specialty fields and related careers to determine whether they are good candidates for postsecondary educational opportunities in engineering.

This course serves as the capstone experience of the high school engineering sequence, integrating technical, professional, and interpersonal skills developed throughout prior coursework. Students work independently and collaboratively to propose, design, execute, document, and present an original engineering solution to a real-world challenge.

docs.title: Target Audience
docs.text: High school students (Honors level)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Engineering II with instructor permission, or Engineering III

docs.title: Main Topics Covered
docs.text:
1. Personal and Professional Qualities — creativity, critical thinking, initiative, integrity, and work ethic
  2. Interpersonal and Team Skills — conflict resolution, communication, diversity, customer service, and collaboration
  3. Professional Competencies — career management, information literacy, information security, current IT systems, mathematical applications, workplace safety, and professionalism
  4. Industry Examination — planning, management, financial responsibility, technical production, technology principles, labor issues, community issues, and health/safety/environmental concerns
  5. Student Organization and Civic Engagement — purposes of student organizations, leadership, membership responsibilities, and internet safety
  6. Work-Based Learning (WBL) — types of WBL, career exploration, and reflective practice
  7. Employability Skills — career research, educational pathways, portfolio development, internship/job application processes, engineering design vs. scientific method
  8. Intellectual Property and Ethics — engineering ethics, intellectual property rights, and professional vs. personal ethics
  9. Practicum Project Design and Execution — systems thinking, engineering design process, proposal development, peer review, project management, scheduling, budgeting, cost estimation, modeling and simulation, risk analysis, and stakeholder communication
  10. Career Preparation — postsecondary research, certifications and licensure, mock interviews, and technical life skills

docs.title: Topics NOT Covered
docs.text:
• Advanced college-level engineering mathematics (calculus, differential equations)
  • Discipline-specific engineering coursework (e.g., civil, electrical, mechanical in depth)
  • Manufacturing or fabrication at an industrial scale
  • Post-secondary coursework content (university-level engineering theory)

  • Demonstrate customer service skills in communicating with project stakeholders

docs.text:

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text: This textbook is organized into 8 chapters covering 85 concepts, aligned to the assignments in Engineering Practicum IV Honors.

docs.title: Chapter Overview
docs.text:
1. Engineering Field Exploration - Research engineering fields, educational pathways, and professional credentials.
  2. Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination - Explore how engineers think and how engineering organizations operate.
  3. Portfolio and Employment Preparation - Build the documents and skills needed to pursue engineering opportunities.
  4. Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP - Define your engineering challenge and understand the ethical and legal responsibilities that guide your work.
  5. Risk Management - Evaluate design safety and analyze risks before committing to a solution.
  6. Project Work Schedule and Budget - Plan the timeline and finances needed to complete your practicum project.
  7. Project Proposal Pitch - Write, present, and peer review your engineering project proposal.
  8. Project Execution, Checkpoints, and Final Presentation - Build, document, and present your completed engineering solution.

docs.title: How to Use This Textbook
docs.text:
Each chapter aligns to one or more assignments in the course. Complete chapters in order — concepts build on each other, and later chapters assume knowledge from earlier ones. Each chapter includes a full list of concepts from the learning graph so you can track your progress.

Note: Each chapter index includes a "TODO: Generate Chapter Content" placeholder. Full content will be added chapter by chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter launches the course by helping students identify and research an engineering field that interests them. Students explore engineering specializations, investigate educational and licensing pathways, and compare certification and degree requirements — building the foundation for every career decision that follows in the course.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text: Engineering Field Exploration Lab — CTE-39, CTE-40, CTE-41, CTE-42

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 11 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Engineering Career Research
  2. Engineering Specializations
  3. Field of Interest Selection
  4. Degree Requirements
  5. Engineering Programs
  6. Postsecondary Education Options
  7. College Admissions Process
  8. Professional Certifications
  9. Licensure Requirements
  10. Professional Engineer (PE) License
  11. Engineer in Training (EIT) Exam

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: This chapter assumes only the prerequisites listed in the course description.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Engineering is not a single career — it is a family of dozens of distinct disciplines, each with its own focus, tools, and professional community. Before you can plan your education or set career goals, you need to understand which branch of engineering fits your interests and strengths. This chapter is your starting point for that exploration.

By the end of this chapter you will be able to name the major engineering specializations, describe the education and licensure path in your chosen field, and explain what the EIT exam and the PE license are and why they matter. All of this feeds directly into your Engineering Field Exploration Lab, where you will research one specific field in depth and present your findings to the class.

docs.title: Engineering Specializations
docs.text:
An engineering specialization is a branch of engineering that focuses on a particular type of problem, technology, or physical system. Each specialization has a professional community, university programs, and a licensing pathway. The table below introduces the most common specializations you will encounter in your research.

Specialization Core Focus Example Projects Civil Engineering Infrastructure: roads, bridges, water systems Highway design, stormwater management Mechanical Engineering Machines, heat transfer, materials Engine design, HVAC systems, robotics Electrical Engineering Circuits, power, signals Power grids, microchips, communication systems Chemical Engineering Chemical processes, materials at scale Pharmaceuticals, fuel production, polymers Computer Engineering Hardware-software integration Embedded systems, computer architecture Environmental Engineering Pollution control, sustainability Water treatment, air quality monitoring Aerospace Engineering Aircraft and spacecraft Commercial jets, satellites, drones Biomedical Engineering Medical devices and systems Prosthetics, imaging equipment, biosensors Industrial Engineering Process efficiency and systems Manufacturing flow, supply chain, ergonomics Software Engineering Software systems and architecture Mobile apps, operating systems, data pipelines

This table is a starting point, not an exhaustive list. There are also emerging specializations — such as materials engineering, nuclear engineering, and systems engineering — that cross the boundaries between the disciplines above.

docs.title: Field of Interest Selection
docs.text:
Choosing a field of interest is not a permanent decision, but it is an important one for this course. Your choice shapes the research you do, the careers you explore, and the lens through which you design your practicum project.

Field of Interest Selection is the process of matching your personal strengths, curiosity, and long-term goals to a specific engineering discipline. Start by asking three questions:

  1. What problems do I find compelling? (Environmental crises, transportation, health care, technology?)
  2. What subjects come most naturally to me? (Biology, physics, computer science, design?)
  3. What does a typical day in this field look like? (Lab work, field work, design work, data analysis?)

Answering these questions honestly will help you narrow your focus before you begin formal research.

docs.title: Engineering Career Research
docs.text:
Once you have a field of interest in mind, the next step is engineering career research — the systematic process of gathering, evaluating, and organizing information about what professionals in that field actually do, how they are compensated, and what pathway leads there.

Effective career research uses multiple source types. Relying on a single source — say, one website or one person's experience — gives you an incomplete picture. The sources below each serve a different purpose:

  • Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) Occupational Outlook Handbook — national salary data, employment projections, and education requirements for every engineering occupation
  • Professional society websites (e.g., ASCE, ASME, IEEE, AIChE) — field-specific news, student membership resources, and career guidance from practicing engineers
  • LinkedIn and job postings — real-world qualifications that employers are asking for right now
  • Informational interviews — conversations with engineers working in your target field, which reveal the day-to-day realities that statistics cannot capture
  • College program pages — what courses you would take and what specializations the program offers

Gathering information is only the first step. Evaluating that information critically — checking publication dates, considering source biases, comparing multiple perspectives — is what makes the research useful.

docs.title: Postsecondary Education Options
docs.text:
Engineering careers almost always require education beyond high school. The term postsecondary education refers to any formal learning that takes place after secondary school (high school). Before choosing a specific program, it helps to understand the full range of options and what each one offers.

The three main postsecondary pathways that lead to engineering careers are described below.

Pathway Credential Typical Duration Engineering Access Community College + Transfer Associate's Degree, then Bachelor's 2 + 2 years Transfer to 4-year engineering program 4-Year University Bachelor of Science (B.S.) 4 years Direct entry to engineering jobs and licensure path Graduate School Master's (M.S.) or Doctorate (Ph.D.) 2–5 years beyond B.S. Research, specialized roles, academia

Community college is a cost-effective way to complete general education requirements before transferring to a four-year engineering program. Many states have guaranteed transfer agreements between community colleges and public universities — check Virginia's Community College System (VCCS) for local examples.

A Bachelor of Science in Engineering (or in a specific discipline like Civil, Electrical, or Mechanical Engineering) is the standard credential for entry-level engineering positions and is required to begin the licensure process. Graduate degrees are optional for many careers but required for advanced research roles and some leadership positions.

docs.title: Engineering Programs and Degree Requirements
docs.text:
An engineering program is a structured curriculum at a college or university that leads to a degree in a specific engineering discipline. Not all programs are equivalent. Accreditation by ABET (Accreditation Board for Engineering and Technology) is the most important quality signal — employers and licensing boards recognize ABET-accredited programs as meeting national standards.

Degree requirements are the specific courses, credit hours, and cumulative GPA thresholds a student must satisfy to earn the degree. While requirements vary by school and discipline, ABET-accredited programs share a common core structure:

  • Mathematics and basic science — typically calculus through differential equations, linear algebra, chemistry, and physics (roughly 30–35% of coursework)
  • Engineering sciences — discipline-specific foundational courses (fluid mechanics, circuit analysis, thermodynamics, etc.)
  • Engineering design — culminating in a senior capstone project that mirrors real professional practice
  • General education — composition, social sciences, humanities
  • Technical electives — specialized courses that let students tailor the degree to a sub-specialty

docs.title: The College Admissions Process
docs.text:
For most students, an engineering degree begins with applying to college. The college admissions process is the set of steps a student takes to apply for admission to a college or university, from researching programs to submitting applications to receiving and comparing offers.

The major steps in a typical four-year university admissions process are:

  1. Research (Junior year and summer before senior year) — Identify 8–12 schools with ABET-accredited programs in your field, considering location, size, cost, and selectivity.
  2. Standardized tests — SAT or ACT scores; some engineering schools also consider SAT Subject Tests or AP exam scores favorably.
  3. Build your application — Transcripts, a personal statement or essays, a resume or activity list, and letters of recommendation from teachers or counselors who know your academic work.
  4. Apply (Fall of senior year) — Submit applications through the Common Application, Coalition Application, or individual school portals. Early Decision and Early Action deadlines are typically November 1–15; Regular Decision deadlines are usually January 1–February 1.
  5. Financial aid — Complete the FAFSA (Free Application for Federal Student Aid) as soon as it opens each year (October 1). Many merit scholarships have separate deadlines.
  6. Decide (Spring of senior year) — Compare acceptance letters and financial aid packages; commit to one school by May 1 (National Decision Day).

Engineering programs are often among the most competitive majors at a given school. Admissions committees look for strength in mathematics and science, evidence of problem-solving ability (coursework, competitions, projects), and genuine interest in the field.

docs.title: Professional Certifications
docs.text:
A professional certification in engineering is a credential awarded by a professional organization that demonstrates a practitioner's competence in a specific area or technology — separate from a government license. Certifications are typically earned after passing an exam and sometimes after accumulating work experience.

Unlike the PE license (which is government-issued and required for certain types of engineering practice), certifications are voluntary but valued by employers. Common examples include:

  • Project Management Professional (PMP) — awarded by PMI; relevant to engineers who manage projects
  • Certified Energy Manager (CEM) — for engineers working in energy efficiency
  • AWS Certified Solutions Architect — for computer and software engineers working with cloud infrastructure
  • LEED AP (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design Accredited Professional) — for civil and environmental engineers involved in sustainable building design

Many professional societies also offer discipline-specific certifications for students and early-career engineers — a good way to demonstrate initiative before you have years of professional experience.

docs.title: Licensure Requirements
docs.text:
Licensure is the legal authorization, issued by a state government, that permits an engineer to offer professional engineering services to the public. While most engineers who work for companies do not legally need a license, licensure is required whenever an engineer:

  • Signs and seals engineering drawings or reports that affect public safety
  • Works as an independent consultant or business owner
  • Works in civil, structural, geotechnical, or certain other public-works fields

The licensure requirements for engineers in the United States follow a four-stage process overseen by the National Council of Examiners for Engineering and Surveying (NCEES) and administered by each state's engineering board.

The four stages are:

  1. Education — Graduate from an ABET-accredited engineering program
  2. Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) Exam — Pass the computer-based exam offered by NCEES (this leads to the EIT credential)
  3. Progressive Experience — Accumulate at least four years of engineering experience under the supervision of a licensed PE
  4. Principles and Practice of Engineering (PE) Exam — Pass the discipline-specific PE exam

docs.title: The Engineer in Training (EIT) Exam
docs.text:
The Engineer in Training (EIT) Exam — officially called the Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) Exam — is the first formal step on the licensure path. It is a computer-based, 5 hour and 20 minute exam administered by NCEES (National Council of Examiners for Engineering and Surveying) at approved testing centers.

Most students take the FE Exam during their final year of college or shortly after graduation, while the foundational mathematics and science concepts are fresh. The exam is offered in several discipline-specific versions, including:

  • FE Chemical
  • FE Civil
  • FE Electrical and Computer
  • FE Environmental
  • FE Industrial and Systems
  • FE Mechanical
  • FE Other Disciplines (a general version)

Passing the FE Exam earns the title Engineer in Training (EIT) or Engineer Intern (EI) depending on the state. The EIT designation is a formal acknowledgment that you have demonstrated competency in the scientific and mathematical foundations of engineering and are ready to begin gaining supervised professional experience.

docs.title: The Professional Engineer (PE) License
docs.text:
The Professional Engineer (PE) License is the highest standard credential in the engineering profession. It is issued by state licensing boards after an engineer demonstrates education, experience, and competence through examination. Once licensed, a PE can stamp engineering documents, take legal responsibility for their designs, and offer engineering services independently.

To receive a PE license, a candidate must have:

  1. A degree from an ABET-accredited program
  2. A passing FE Exam score (EIT status)
  3. Four years of progressive engineering experience under the direct supervision of a PE
  4. A passing score on the PE Exam in their discipline

The PE Exam is an 8-hour exam (split into morning and afternoon sessions) administered by NCEES twice per year. It tests advanced, discipline-specific knowledge — significantly deeper than the FE Exam.

docs.title: Comparing Certifications and Licensure
docs.text:
It is easy to confuse professional certifications with professional licensure. Both are credentials that signal competence, but they serve different functions and carry different legal weight. The following comparison clarifies the distinction.

Feature Professional Certification PE License Issuing authority Professional association or vendor State government board Legal requirement Voluntary Required for certain public-safety work Exam Association-specific NCEES FE Exam → PE Exam Work experience required Varies (often 2–5 years) 4 years under PE supervision Renewal Annual or biennial, with continuing education Biennial, with PDH hours Portability National or international State-by-state (reciprocity agreements exist)

Many engineers hold both certifications and a PE license — the certifications signal expertise in a specialty area, while the PE license signals professional accountability and legal authority.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
This chapter introduced the landscape of engineering specializations and the pathways that lead from high school into professional engineering practice. Here is what you should be able to recall and explain after working through this material:

  • Engineering has dozens of specializations; your field of interest is where your skills, curiosity, and the world's needs intersect.
  • Career research requires multiple source types — government data, professional societies, real job postings, and conversations with practitioners.
  • Postsecondary options range from community college transfer tracks to four-year programs to graduate school, each with different timelines and costs.
  • ABET accreditation is the quality standard for engineering degree programs — check for it before committing to a school.
  • The licensure path follows a fixed sequence: ABET degree → FE Exam (EIT) → four years of supervised experience → PE Exam → PE License.
  • Professional certifications are voluntary, specialty-area credentials that complement the PE license but do not replace it.

Your Engineering Field Exploration Lab will ask you to apply all of these concepts to one specific engineering field. Come to that assignment with a field already in mind — the self-assessment explorer above is a good starting point if you are undecided.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Two of the most important thinking frameworks in the modern world are the scientific method and the engineering design process. At first glance, they seem similar — both are systematic, both involve testing ideas, and both drive innovation. Yet they answer fundamentally different questions. Science asks why something happens. Engineering asks how we can make something happen, or how we can make something work better.

This chapter unpacks both frameworks, compares them side by side, and then introduces a third lens — systems thinking — that engineers use to understand the organizations and industries they work within. All three frameworks will appear throughout your practicum project, from the moment you identify a problem to the day you present your final solution.

docs.title: The Scientific Method
docs.text:
The scientific method is a structured process for generating reliable knowledge about the natural world through observation, experimentation, and evidence-based reasoning. It has been refined over centuries and forms the backbone of all natural and social sciences.

The classical steps of the scientific method are:

  1. Observation — Notice a phenomenon or ask a question about the natural world.
  2. Background Research — Gather existing knowledge related to the question.
  3. Hypothesis — Propose a testable, falsifiable explanation for the observed phenomenon.
  4. Experiment Design — Plan a controlled experiment to test the hypothesis.
  5. Data Collection — Conduct the experiment and record measurements systematically.
  6. Analysis — Examine the data to identify patterns and draw conclusions.
  7. Communication — Share findings with the scientific community for peer review and replication.

The method is inherently circular: conclusions from one experiment often generate new questions, launching the cycle again. This iterative nature is what allows science to self-correct over time.

docs.title: Hypothesis Testing
docs.text:
Before we move to engineering, it is worth pausing on one of the most important steps in the scientific method: hypothesis testing. A hypothesis is a specific, testable prediction about a relationship between variables. The key word is testable — a hypothesis must be capable of being proven wrong by evidence, a property philosophers of science call falsifiability.

A well-formed hypothesis follows the "If … then … because …" structure:

  • If the independent variable is changed in a specific way,
  • then the dependent variable will respond in a predictable way,
  • because a known mechanism or principle explains the connection.

Example: "If we double the insulation thickness on a pipe, then heat loss will decrease by at least 40%, because heat conduction is inversely proportional to material thickness."

Scientists compare results against a null hypothesis — the assumption that no relationship exists — and use statistical tests to determine whether the evidence is strong enough to reject it. Engineers sometimes borrow hypothesis-testing language when running controlled experiments during prototyping, but their goal is usually practical performance rather than knowledge generation.

docs.title: Diagram: Hypothesis Testing Decision Tree
docs.text:
Interactive Hypothesis Testing Decision Tree

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: hypothesis-testing-tree Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will explain (Bloom L2) the logical structure of hypothesis testing by navigating an interactive decision tree that traces a hypothesis from formation through accept/reject outcome.

Canvas: 800 × 480 px, responsive to window resize.

Layout: A vertical flowchart with six node levels. Each node is a rounded rectangle with a label and a brief description (visible on hover). Directional arrows connect nodes. Two branch paths diverge at the "Analyze Results" node — one leading to "Reject Null Hypothesis" (green) and one to "Fail to Reject Null Hypothesis" (amber).

Nodes (top to bottom): 1. Observe a Phenomenon 2. Form a Hypothesis (If/Then/Because) 3. Design a Controlled Experiment 4. Collect Data 5. Analyze Results → branch point - Left branch: Evidence is statistically significant → Reject Null Hypothesis → New Knowledge Generated - Right branch: Evidence is not significant → Fail to Reject Null Hypothesis → Revise Hypothesis

Interaction: Clicking any node opens a side panel (200 px wide, right side) showing: (a) the step name, (b) a 2-sentence explanation at Senior High reading level, and (c) a real-world engineering example of that step.

Color scheme: Nodes are SteelBlue; the two outcome nodes use DarkGreen (reject) and DarkGoldenrod (fail to reject). Arrows are dark gray.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/hypothesis-testing-tree/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 520 px height.

docs.title: The Engineering Design Process
docs.text:
The engineering design process is a systematic, iterative approach for solving problems and creating artifacts — products, structures, systems, or processes — that meet a defined set of criteria and constraints. Unlike the scientific method, which seeks to understand how the world is, the engineering design process seeks to change how the world works.

The most widely adopted version of the process includes these steps:

  1. Define the Problem — Identify the need or opportunity. Articulate criteria (what the solution must do) and constraints (limits on time, budget, materials, or safety).
  2. Research — Gather background information, study existing solutions, and identify relevant standards.
  3. Brainstorm Solutions — Generate a wide range of possible approaches without judgment.
  4. Select a Solution — Evaluate options against criteria and constraints; choose the most promising approach.
  5. Prototype — Build a working model or representation of the chosen solution.
  6. Test and Evaluate — Test the prototype against the criteria and constraints; measure performance.
  7. Iterate — Use test results to improve the design; repeat until the solution meets requirements.
  8. Communicate — Document the solution and present it to stakeholders.

The most critical word in that list is iterate. Real engineering design rarely moves in a straight line from problem to solution. Engineers cycle back — sometimes many times — as testing reveals weaknesses or new constraints emerge.

docs.title: Engineering vs. Science: A Direct Comparison
docs.text:
Understanding how these two frameworks differ is essential for recognizing which one to apply — and when. In practice, engineers use elements of both, but with different primary goals.

Dimension Scientific Method Engineering Design Process Primary question Why does this happen? How can we make this work? Goal Generate verified knowledge Create a solution that meets criteria Starting point Observation of a natural phenomenon Identified need or problem Key output Knowledge (publications, theories) Artifact (product, system, process) Test criteria Statistical significance Performance against criteria/constraints "Success" means Hypothesis supported or falsified Solution meets requirements Iteration purpose Refine understanding Improve design performance Community check Peer review and replication Engineering review and standards compliance

One important nuance: engineering often depends on science. When a civil engineer designs a bridge, they rely on scientific knowledge about material properties, fluid dynamics, and soil mechanics. But the design decisions — how long, how wide, what materials, what cost — are engineering judgments, not scientific conclusions.

docs.title: Diagram: Venn Diagram — Scientific Method vs. Engineering Design Process
docs.text:
Interactive Venn Diagram: Scientific Method vs. Engineering Design Process

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: science-vs-engineering-venn Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will differentiate (Bloom L4) the unique and shared features of the scientific method and engineering design process by clicking on regions of a Venn diagram to reveal categorized content.

Canvas: 800 × 420 px, responsive.

Layout: Two large overlapping circles. Left circle labeled "Scientific Method" (SteelBlue). Right circle labeled "Engineering Design Process" (DarkGoldenrod). Overlapping region is a medium gray labeled "Both".

Clickable regions (three): - Left-only region: Opens a panel listing 4 unique features of the scientific method (e.g., "Falsifiable hypothesis required," "Statistical analysis of data," "Peer-reviewed publication," "Seeks universal laws"). - Overlap region: Opens a panel listing 4 shared features (e.g., "Systematic process," "Iterative in practice," "Requires background research," "Builds on prior work"). - Right-only region: Opens a panel listing 4 unique features of the engineering design process (e.g., "Defined by criteria and constraints," "Produces a physical or digital artifact," "Optimized for performance," "Judged by standards compliance").

Panel: Appears to the right of the Venn diagram (200 px wide). Each list item is a short bullet. Clicking a different region replaces the panel content with a smooth fade.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/science-vs-engineering-venn/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 460 px height.

docs.title: Systems Thinking
docs.text:
Once you understand how engineers design solutions, the next question is: how do those solutions fit into larger organizations and industries? The answer requires systems thinking — a way of looking at problems by examining the whole system and the relationships between its parts, rather than focusing on individual components in isolation.

A system is a set of interacting or interdependent components that form a unified whole and produce behaviors that none of the components could produce alone. Systems thinking shifts the engineer's attention from "what is this part?" to "how do the parts interact, and what emerges from those interactions?"

Systems thinking is particularly powerful when:

  • A problem has multiple causes that reinforce each other
  • A solution in one area creates unexpected problems somewhere else
  • The behavior of a system changes over time in non-obvious ways
  • Stakeholders disagree about the root cause of a problem

Engineers who think in systems are better at designing solutions that do not create new problems downstream — a skill that becomes especially important when you are designing for public health, safety, or the environment.

docs.title: System Inputs and Outputs
docs.text:
Every system, no matter how complex, can be described in terms of what goes in, what happens inside, and what comes out. System inputs are the resources, information, or energy that enter the system. System outputs are the products, services, information, or waste that the system produces.

Consider a water treatment plant as a system:

Component Examples Inputs Raw water, chemicals (chlorine, alum), electricity, labor Processes Filtration, sedimentation, disinfection, pH adjustment Outputs Clean drinking water (desired), sludge (waste), treated wastewater Environment Regulatory standards, local geography, seasonal variation

Every engineering system also has a boundary — the line that separates what is inside the system from what is outside. Choosing the right boundary is a design decision: a boundary that is too narrow ignores important interactions; one that is too wide makes the analysis unmanageable.

docs.title: Feedback Loops
docs.text:
One of the most important concepts in systems thinking is the feedback loop — a mechanism in which outputs of a system circle back to influence its own inputs or internal processes. Feedback loops explain why systems often behave in surprising, non-linear ways.

There are two fundamental types of feedback loops:

Balancing (negative) feedback loops resist change and push the system back toward a target or equilibrium. They are stabilizing. A thermostat is the classic example: when room temperature rises above the set point, the system turns off the heater, which causes the temperature to fall back toward the target.

Reinforcing (positive) feedback loops amplify change and drive the system further from its starting point. They can be either virtuous (compounding growth) or vicious (accelerating collapse). Corrosion on a metal bridge is an example: exposure to moisture causes initial rusting, which makes the surface rougher and more porous, which accelerates further corrosion.

Identifying feedback loops in your practicum project's environment — financial, social, technical — will help you anticipate how your solution will behave after implementation.

docs.title: Diagram: Feedback Loop Simulator
docs.text:
Interactive Feedback Loop Simulator

Type: MicroSim sim-id: feedback-loop-simulator Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) feedback loop concepts by adjusting parameters in a simulated system and observing how balancing and reinforcing loops produce different patterns of change over time.

Canvas: 800 × 500 px, responsive.

System simulated: A simplified project budget system. The state variable is "Budget Remaining" (starts at $10,000). Two sliders control the system: - Spending Rate (slider: $100–$1,000 per week) — a drain on budget - Correction Strength (slider: 0–1.0) — how aggressively a manager reduces spending when budget falls below a warning threshold ($3,000)

Left panel (controls, 250 px): - Spending Rate slider with label and current value - Correction Strength slider with label and current value - Loop Type radio buttons: "Balancing Only," "Reinforcing Only," "Both" - Reset button

Right panel (visualization, 550 px): A line chart (Chart.js embedded in p5 canvas or separate div) showing Budget Remaining over 52 weeks. The x-axis is Week (0–52); the y-axis is Budget ($0–$12,000). A horizontal dashed red line marks the Warning Threshold at $3,000.

When "Balancing Only" is selected: the line stabilizes around the threshold as the correction kicks in (S-curve behavior). When "Reinforcing Only" is selected: spending accelerates as budget falls (the project manager panics and spends more trying to fix problems), producing a rapid drop to zero. When "Both" is selected: the system oscillates.

Interaction: Sliders update the simulation in real time. Hovering over any point on the line reveals a tooltip with the exact week and budget value. Clicking on the chart pauses the animation; clicking again resumes.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/feedback-loop-simulator/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 560 px height.

docs.title: Subsystem Interactions
docs.text:
Large engineering systems are rarely designed as single monolithic units. Instead, they are broken into subsystems — smaller, semi-independent components that each perform a specific function and interact with each other through defined interfaces.

Subsystem interactions are the connections, data flows, energy transfers, and material flows that pass between subsystems. Understanding how subsystems interact is essential because:

  • A change in one subsystem can propagate through interfaces and affect others unexpectedly
  • The performance of the overall system depends on how well subsystems communicate and cooperate
  • Failures at the interface between subsystems are among the most common causes of engineering disasters

Consider an electric vehicle (EV) as a system of interacting subsystems:

Subsystem Function Key Interfaces Battery Pack Store electrical energy Electrical connection to motor controller Motor Controller Regulate power delivery Electrical (battery), mechanical (motor), data (sensors) Drive Motor Convert electrical energy to motion Mechanical connection to drivetrain Thermal Management Maintain battery temperature Heat exchanger, coolant loop, sensors Regenerative Braking Recover kinetic energy as electricity Mechanical (brakes), electrical (battery charging circuit) Driver Interface Communicate state to driver Data bus, display, controls

Notice that the thermal management subsystem interacts with the battery pack, the motor, and the environment — a change in ambient temperature affects multiple subsystems simultaneously. This web of interactions is exactly what systems thinking is designed to help you navigate.

docs.title: Applying These Frameworks to the Scavenger Hunt
docs.text:
Your Engineering Industry Scavenger Hunt asks you to investigate a real engineering industry or organization and find evidence of the frameworks introduced in this chapter. As you research your assigned industry, use these questions as a guide:

  • Where does this industry use the scientific method? (R&D labs, testing, regulatory submissions)
  • Where does it use the engineering design process? (Product development, construction, system upgrades)
  • What are the major inputs and outputs of this industry as a system?
  • What feedback loops can you identify? (Quality control, market signals, regulatory responses)
  • What are the major subsystems and how do they interact?

Documenting your answers — with real examples from your chosen industry — will prepare you to write a sharp Engineering Design Process vs. Scientific Method Comparison and to present your findings to the class.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
The two foundational frameworks introduced in this chapter will appear in every engineering course you take from here forward:

  • The scientific method is a process for generating knowledge about the natural world through observation, hypothesis testing, and evidence-based reasoning.
  • A hypothesis must be falsifiable and is tested against a null hypothesis using controlled experiments and statistical analysis.
  • The engineering design process is a systematic, iterative approach to creating solutions that meet defined criteria and constraints — it produces artifacts, not knowledge.
  • Engineering and science are complementary: engineering relies on scientific knowledge, but its goal is problem-solving rather than knowledge generation.
  • Systems thinking views problems through the lens of interacting components, boundaries, inputs, outputs, and emergent behavior.
  • System inputs and outputs define what enters and leaves a system; identifying them is the first step in analyzing any engineering organization.
  • Feedback loops — balancing (stabilizing) and reinforcing (amplifying) — explain how systems respond to change over time.
  • Subsystem interactions are the interfaces between semi-independent components; failures at those interfaces are a major source of engineering problems.

Carry these frameworks into every chapter ahead. When you encounter a new engineering challenge, your first instinct should be to ask: Am I doing science or engineering? And what system am I working within?

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter prepares students to enter the engineering job market by building the core documents and skills employers look for — a professional resume, a targeted cover letter, and a complete work portfolio. It culminates in a mock interview experience that puts all of these materials to use in a realistic professional setting.

docs.title: Assignments
docs.text:
• Portfolio Development — CTE-43, CTE-21
  • Cover Letter — CTE-44, CTE-43, CTE-21
  • Resume — CTE-44, CTE-43, CTE-21
  • Professional Mock Interview Event — CTE-65

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 8 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Resume Writing
  2. Cover Letter Writing
  3. Work Portfolio
  4. Portfolio Components
  5. Job Application Process
  6. Engineering Internships
  7. Interview Skills
  8. Mock Interview Practice

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Getting hired in engineering requires more than technical knowledge. Employers evaluate candidates on how clearly they communicate their skills, how professionally they present themselves, and how well they perform in high-pressure conversations. This chapter builds those three pillars: a portfolio that shows your work, application documents that tell your story, and interview skills that let you deliver it confidently.

Everything you create in this chapter has a second purpose: it is also the beginning of a professional record you will carry into college, internships, and beyond. The habit of documenting your work — what you built, what you learned, what you would do differently — is one of the most underrated skills in the engineering profession.

docs.title: Your Work Portfolio
docs.text:
A work portfolio is a curated collection of materials that demonstrates your skills, experiences, and growth as an engineering student. Unlike a resume, which summarizes your background in one page, a portfolio shows your work rather than just describing it. Employers and college admissions officers use portfolios to see how you think and what you are capable of, not just what titles you have held.

Portfolios exist in two formats. A physical portfolio is a binder or folder containing printed documents, photos, and artifacts. A digital portfolio is a website, PDF, or platform-hosted collection (e.g., Google Sites, Wix, Adobe Portfolio) that can be shared via a link. Digital portfolios are increasingly preferred in engineering because they are easy to update, shareable, and can include video and interactive content.

The most effective portfolios are organized around outcomes — what you achieved — rather than activities alone. "Designed and tested three bridge prototypes" is stronger than "Participated in a bridge project."

docs.title: Portfolio Components
docs.text:
Portfolio components are the specific types of materials that go inside a work portfolio. Including a variety of component types demonstrates breadth. The following categories are standard for engineering students:

Component What It Shows Example Personal statement Goals, values, and professional identity "Why I want to be a civil engineer" Resume Summary of education, skills, experience One-page formatted resume Project descriptions Problem-solving process and outcomes Design brief + photos of prototype Academic work samples Technical depth and writing ability Lab report, CAD drawings, calculations Certifications and awards Recognized achievements OSHA 10 certificate, FIRST robotics award Reflections Growth mindset and self-awareness "What I learned from a failed design" Letters of recommendation External validation of your skills Teacher or mentor letter Contact information Professional reachability Email, LinkedIn, portfolio URL

Not every student will have all of these components right now — and that is fine. The goal at this stage is to gather what you have and identify the gaps you want to fill before graduation.

docs.title: Diagram: Portfolio Component Checklist Builder
docs.text:
Interactive Portfolio Component Checklist Builder

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: portfolio-checklist-builder Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will evaluate (Bloom L5) the completeness of their current portfolio by checking off components they have, identifying gaps, and receiving a readiness score.

Canvas: 800 × 520 px, responsive to window resize.

Left panel (checklist — 320 px): Each portfolio component appears as a labeled checkbox row. When checked, the row turns green. A progress bar at the bottom tracks the number of components checked out of the total.

Right panel (readiness gauge — 480 px): A semicircular gauge (like a speedometer) labeled "Portfolio Readiness." The needle moves from 0% to 100% as components are checked. Three zones are color-coded:

  • 0–40%: Red — "Getting started"
  • 41–70%: Gold — "Making progress"
  • 71–100%: Green — "Strong portfolio"

Below the gauge, a dynamic text area lists the unchecked components as "Next steps to strengthen your portfolio."

Interaction: Clicking any checkbox immediately updates the gauge and the next-steps list. Hovering over a component name shows a one-sentence tooltip explaining why that component matters to employers.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/portfolio-checklist-builder/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Resume Writing
docs.text:
A resume is a one-page professional document that summarizes your education, skills, work experience, and relevant achievements for a specific audience — typically an employer or college admissions officer. The word "résumé" comes from French meaning "summary," which captures exactly what it is: a tight, curated version of your professional story.

Engineering resumes follow a standard structure. Before we look at the structure, two vocabulary terms need clear definitions. Quantifiable results are achievements expressed with numbers ("increased efficiency by 20%," "designed a structure that held 47 lbs"). Action verbs are strong, specific verbs that begin bullet points to show what you did rather than what you were responsible for — words like designed, analyzed, built, tested, optimized, and presented.

The standard engineering resume sections are:

  • Header — Name, phone, email, and portfolio or LinkedIn URL (no home address required)
  • Education — School name, expected graduation year, GPA if 3.5 or above, relevant coursework
  • Skills — Technical skills (CAD software, programming languages, lab equipment) and professional skills
  • Experience — Internships, jobs, or significant volunteer roles; each entry has 2–4 bullet points beginning with action verbs
  • Projects — Class or personal projects that demonstrate engineering skills (often the most important section for students)
  • Awards and Activities — Competitions, student organizations, certifications

A one-page rule applies to students and early-career engineers. Hiring managers spend an average of 6–7 seconds scanning a resume before deciding whether to read it closely. White space, consistent formatting, and a clean font (11–12 pt, standard fonts like Calibri or Arial) are not cosmetic concerns — they are functional ones.

Resume Mistake Why It Hurts Using "responsible for" instead of action verbs Sounds passive; doesn't show what you actually did Missing quantifiable results Claims aren't credible without numbers Inconsistent formatting (mixed fonts, sizes) Signals lack of attention to detail Including a photo or personal info (age, gender) Unprofessional in the U.S. context; creates bias risk Generic objective statement Wastes space; every applicant wants "a challenging position" More than one page (for students) Suggests poor judgment about what matters

docs.title: Cover Letter Writing
docs.text:
A cover letter is a one-page business letter submitted alongside a resume. Its purpose is not to repeat the resume — it is to explain why you are applying to this specific position and why you are the right fit. A cover letter gives you space to tell a story that bullet points cannot.

The three-paragraph structure works for most engineering cover letters:

  1. Opening paragraph — State the specific position you are applying for, how you learned about it, and one sentence that immediately signals your strongest qualification. Do not start with "My name is..." — the reader already knows that from the header.
  2. Body paragraph — Connect two or three of your specific experiences (from your resume or portfolio) to the requirements listed in the job posting. Use concrete examples, not generalities.
  3. Closing paragraph — Thank the reader for their time, express enthusiasm for the opportunity, and include a specific call to action: "I would welcome the opportunity to discuss how my experience in [X] could contribute to [Company Y]'s team."

The most common cover letter mistake is writing the same generic letter for every application. Employers notice immediately when a letter is not customized. Look at the job posting carefully: reuse the company's own vocabulary, reference a specific project or value from their website, and address the letter to a named person if you can find one.

docs.title: The Job Application Process
docs.text:
The job application process is the sequence of steps between identifying a job opening and receiving an employment offer. Understanding each step reduces anxiety and increases the chance of success. For engineering internships and entry-level positions, the typical sequence is:

  1. Job search — Identify openings through job boards (LinkedIn, Indeed, Handshake), company websites, professional society job boards (ASCE, ASME, IEEE), and networking.
  2. Tailoring application materials — Customize your resume and cover letter for each position; adjust the skills section to match keywords in the job description.
  3. Submission — Apply through the company's online portal; upload resume and cover letter in PDF format; complete any online screening questions.
  4. Application tracking system (ATS) screening — Many companies use software to scan applications for keywords before a human reads them. Using exact language from the job posting helps your application pass this filter.
  5. Phone or video screening — A 15–30 minute call with a recruiter to verify basic qualifications and interest.
  6. Technical interview or assessment — Some engineering employers assign a take-home problem or a coding/design challenge.
  7. Panel or on-site interview — One or more in-person or video interviews with hiring managers and team members.
  8. Offer and negotiation — If selected, you receive a formal offer letter; for internships, most students accept the first offer, but it is always appropriate to ask about start dates and housing stipends.

docs.title: Diagram: Job Application Process Flowchart
docs.text:
Interactive Job Application Process Flowchart

Type: workflow diagram sim-id: job-application-flowchart Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will recall (Bloom L1) the correct sequence of the job application process and apply (Bloom L3) it by identifying where in the process a given scenario falls.

Canvas: 800 × 520 px, responsive.

Layout: Eight rectangular nodes arranged in a vertical flowchart with connecting arrows. Each node is labeled with a step name and numbered. Nodes are color-coded by phase: blue (search/prep), gold (submission), green (interviews), teal (offer).

Interaction: Clicking any node opens a side panel (300 px wide) showing: - Step name and number - A 2–3 sentence description of what happens at this step - One student tip for succeeding at this step - An icon representing the action (search icon, document icon, phone icon, etc.)

A "where am I?" quiz mode: A button at the bottom labeled "Test Yourself" presents a brief scenario sentence (e.g., "You just received an email from a recruiter asking for a 20-minute call. What step are you in?") and the student clicks the correct node. The node flashes green for correct or red for incorrect, with a brief explanation.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/job-application-flowchart/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Engineering Internships
docs.text:
An engineering internship is a structured, paid work experience — typically 10–12 weeks during the summer — in which a student works at an engineering firm, company, or government agency under the supervision of professional engineers. Internships are one of the most important steps you can take as an engineering student because they:

  • Give you real project experience that strengthens your resume and portfolio
  • Confirm (or challenge) your interest in the field before you commit to a career path
  • Build your professional network at a time when contacts are especially valuable
  • Frequently convert into full-time job offers — according to the National Association of Colleges and Employers (NACE), about 70% of interns who perform well receive a full-time offer from the same employer

Most engineering internships are available to students who have completed at least their freshman year of college, but some companies — including NASA, Google, and many defense contractors — offer high school internship and shadowing programs. The Virginia Governor's Opportunity to Work (GO Virginia) initiative and programs like NYLF Engineering are worth researching now.

When evaluating internship opportunities, consider four factors beyond the hourly wage:

  • Mentorship quality — Will you work directly with engineers, or will you mainly do administrative tasks?
  • Project ownership — Will you contribute to a real deliverable, or just observe?
  • Location and logistics — Remote, in-person, or hybrid? Housing stipend provided?
  • Alignment with your field — Does the company's work match your engineering specialization?

docs.title: Interview Skills
docs.text:
Interview skills are the communication and professional behaviors that allow a candidate to effectively present their qualifications, experience, and personality during a hiring conversation. Unlike resumes and cover letters — which you prepare in advance — interviews require real-time performance under pressure.

The most widely taught framework for answering behavioral interview questions is the STAR method. Before looking at the framework, here are the four terms it uses:

  • Situation — The context or background of a specific event
  • Task — What you were responsible for in that situation
  • Action — The specific steps you took
  • Result — What happened because of those steps (quantified when possible)

A behavioral interview question asks you to describe a past experience: "Tell me about a time you faced a technical challenge you didn't know how to solve." A STAR-structured answer sounds like: "In my Engineering III class (Situation), my team's bridge design failed the first load test and we had less than a week to redesign it (Task). I analyzed the failure points and proposed switching from a truss to an arch design based on research I did overnight (Action). Our redesigned bridge held 50% more weight than the original and we passed the assignment (Result)."

Preparation for interviews also includes behavioral expectations that are evaluated before you say a word:

Professional Behavior Standard Arrival time 5–10 minutes early; never late Attire Business casual minimum; business professional preferred Handshake and eye contact Firm, brief; maintain comfortable eye contact (not a stare) Phone Silent and out of sight for the entire interview Note-taking Bringing a notepad signals engagement; taking notes is appropriate Asking questions Always have 2–3 thoughtful questions ready for the end

docs.title: Mock Interview Practice
docs.text:
Mock interview practice is a rehearsal exercise in which a student simulates a real job interview with a teacher, classmate, or community volunteer serving as the interviewer. Research on interview performance consistently shows that students who practice aloud — not just mentally — perform significantly better than those who only think through their answers.

Your Professional Mock Interview Event will be structured like a real interview: business dress, formal introductions, a set of behavioral and technical questions, and written feedback from the interviewer. Treat this exactly as you would treat a real opportunity.

Preparation steps before your mock interview:

  1. Research the "company" — Read the job description you have been assigned; understand what the role requires.
  2. Prepare STAR stories — Write out three to five experiences from your resume or portfolio in STAR format. Each story should be usable for multiple question types.
  3. Practice aloud — Speak your answers out loud, not just in your head. Timing matters: most answers should be 90 seconds to 2 minutes.
  4. Prepare your questions — Write two or three genuine questions about the role or company. Generic questions ("What is the salary?") are a red flag.
  5. Dress and logistics — Lay out your clothes the night before; bring a copy of your resume and portfolio.

After the mock interview, the feedback you receive is as valuable as the practice itself. Review every comment and identify one or two specific behaviors to change before your next interview opportunity.

docs.title: Diagram: STAR Answer Builder
docs.text:
Interactive STAR Answer Builder

Type: MicroSim sim-id: star-answer-builder Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) the STAR method by constructing a structured interview answer from their own experience using a guided four-step form.

Canvas: 800 × 560 px, responsive.

Layout: Four vertically stacked input cards, each labeled with one STAR element (Situation, Task, Action, Result). Each card has: - A 3–4 word prompt label in bold (e.g., "SITUATION — Set the scene") - A text area (3 lines) for the student's input - A character counter showing recommended length (Situation: 30–60 words; Task: 20–40 words; Action: 50–80 words; Result: 30–50 words) - A color indicator that turns green when the word count is in the recommended range

Preview panel (right side — 300 px): As the student types, a "How it sounds" preview assembles the four pieces into a flowing paragraph. Transition phrases are automatically inserted ("In this situation…", "My responsibility was to…", "I responded by…", "As a result…") so students can hear how the pieces connect.

Bottom button — "Practice Mode": Hides the STAR labels and shows only the assembled paragraph so the student can read it aloud as practice. A timer counts up to help students gauge their pacing.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/star-answer-builder/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 620 px height.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
This chapter built the three pillars of employment preparation that every engineering student needs:

  • A work portfolio is a curated collection of your best work — it shows what you can do, not just what you have done. Start building it now with projects, academic work samples, and reflections.
  • Portfolio components span eight categories from personal statements to letters of recommendation; a strong portfolio samples from most of them.
  • A resume is a one-page, action-verb-driven summary of your qualifications. Quantify your results whenever possible and format for a 6-second scan.
  • A cover letter explains why you want this specific job and connects your experience to the employer's needs — it is never a copy of your resume.
  • The job application process follows a defined sequence from search to offer; understanding each step — including ATS screening — makes the process less mysterious.
  • Engineering internships offer real project experience, professional networking, and a high conversion rate to full-time employment.
  • Interview skills are practiced, not innate. The STAR method gives you a reliable structure for any behavioral question.
  • Mock interview practice is the single most effective preparation you can do before a real interview — take the event seriously and mine the feedback deeply.

All four assignments in this chapter — portfolio, cover letter, resume, and mock interview — feed each other. A strong portfolio makes the resume more credible; a sharp resume makes the cover letter easier to write; and strong application documents give you confidence in the interview.

docs.title: Chapter 4 - Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP
Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP
Summary
docs.text: This is the launch chapter for the practicum project. Students identify a real engineering need or opportunity, define their challenge, and begin applying project management thinking to their solution. Woven throughout is an introduction to the ethical and legal responsibilities engineers carry — including professional ethics, intellectual property rights, and the obligations engineers have to the public — framed as essential context for making good design decisions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration
  • Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Every engineering project begins not with a solution, but with a problem. Before you can design anything, you need to clearly understand what challenge you are trying to address, who is affected by it, and what limitations your solution must work within. This chapter walks you through that process — from noticing a problem in the world around you to writing a focused engineering challenge definition that will anchor your practicum project.

But defining a good problem is only part of what engineers do at the start of a project. Engineers also carry significant responsibilities: to their clients, to the public, and to the profession itself. This chapter introduces the ethical codes that govern engineering practice and the intellectual property laws that protect creative and technical work. Understanding both sets of responsibilities will help you make better decisions as you design and document your project.

docs.title: Problem Identification
docs.text:
Problem identification is the process of recognizing that a gap exists between how things currently work and how they could or should work. In engineering, a problem is not simply an annoyance — it is an observable condition that causes harm, inefficiency, or unmet need for a specific group of people or a system.

Engineers find problems by observing the world carefully, listening to the people who experience a gap, and asking questions like:

  • Who is affected, and how often?
  • What is the cost — in time, money, safety, or quality of life — of leaving this problem unsolved?
  • Has someone already tried to solve it? Why did that approach fall short?

Not every frustration is a viable engineering problem. A problem worth engineering has a measurable impact, a definable scope, and at least the possibility of a designed solution.

docs.title: Needs Assessment
docs.text:
Once you have identified a candidate problem, the next step is a needs assessment — a structured investigation that helps you confirm the problem is real, understand it fully, and determine whether it is worth solving. A needs assessment typically involves four activities:

  1. Stakeholder interviews — talking directly to people who experience the problem
  2. Data collection — gathering numbers that quantify the impact (how many people, how often, what cost)
  3. Root cause analysis — asking "why does this problem exist?" repeatedly until you reach the underlying cause rather than a surface symptom
  4. Literature review — researching whether existing solutions exist and why they may be insufficient

The output of a needs assessment is a clear statement of the gap: what is happening now, what should be happening, and what is preventing the improvement.

docs.title: Opportunity Recognition
docs.text:
Problems and opportunities are two sides of the same coin. Opportunity recognition is the skill of reframing a problem as a specific, actionable opportunity for a designed solution. This shift in framing matters because it moves your thinking from what is wrong to what could be built.

Compare these two framings of the same situation:

Problem Frame Opportunity Frame "Students at our school waste time looking for open parking spaces." "An opportunity exists to design a low-cost sensor system that displays real-time parking availability on a map." "Elderly residents in this neighborhood can't read food labels easily." "An opportunity exists to develop a smartphone app that reads and enlarges nutrition information using the phone's camera." "The school's garden irrigation wastes water during rainy weeks." "An opportunity exists to build an automated irrigation controller that checks a weather API before opening the valves."

Notice that the opportunity frame names a specific intervention and a specific beneficiary. This specificity is what makes the opportunity actionable.

docs.title: Engineering Challenge Definition
docs.text:
An engineering challenge definition (sometimes called a problem statement) is a concise, written statement that captures the problem, the stakeholders affected, the measurable impact, and the constraints and goals for a potential solution. It is the single most important document you will write at the start of your project, because every design decision you make later should trace back to it.

A well-written engineering challenge definition has four components:

  1. The situation — a one-sentence description of the current state and who is affected
  2. The need — a clear statement of what is missing or insufficient
  3. The impact — a quantified description of the cost of the problem
  4. The goal — what a successful solution would accomplish, expressed in measurable terms

Example:

Students at [School Name] spend an average of 8 minutes per day searching for open parking spots during peak arrival hours, resulting in tardiness for approximately 30 students per week. A successful solution would reduce average search time to under 2 minutes without requiring students to download an app or access the school's Wi-Fi network.

This statement is specific, measurable, and does not prescribe a particular solution — it leaves the design space open.

docs.title: Constraints and Criteria
docs.text:
Before brainstorming solutions, engineers define the boundaries within which any solution must operate. These boundaries take two forms: constraints and criteria.

A constraint is a non-negotiable limit — a condition that any valid solution must satisfy. Constraints come from physics, safety regulations, available materials, time, budget, or the needs of stakeholders. If a solution violates a constraint, it is not a viable solution regardless of its other merits.

A criterion (plural: criteria) is a desired quality — a goal the solution should achieve as much as possible. Unlike constraints, criteria can be partially satisfied. They are used to compare solutions and choose between alternatives.

Type Definition Example Constraint Must be satisfied (binary — yes or no) Solution must cost less than $200 to build Constraint Must be satisfied Solution must not require a WiFi connection Criterion Should be maximized or optimized Solution should be as lightweight as possible Criterion Should be maximized or optimized Solution should be usable by people of all ages

Listing your constraints and criteria before you brainstorm keeps your design process honest — it prevents you from falling in love with an idea that cannot actually work.

docs.title: Brainstorming Solutions
docs.text:
Brainstorming is a structured ideation technique for generating a large number of candidate solutions before evaluating any of them. The critical rule of productive brainstorming is deferred judgment: during the generative phase, no idea is criticized or eliminated. Premature evaluation kills creative thinking.

Effective brainstorming techniques for engineering include:

  • Mind mapping — starting with the engineering challenge at the center and branching outward into different solution categories
  • SCAMPER — systematically asking whether existing solutions can be Substituted, Combined, Adapted, Modified, Put to other uses, Eliminated, or Reversed
  • Crazy eights — sketching eight rough solution concepts in eight minutes to push past obvious first ideas
  • Biomimicry prompting — asking "how does nature solve a similar problem?"

After the generative phase, you evaluate candidates against your constraints and criteria — eliminating those that violate constraints and ranking the rest by how well they satisfy the criteria. The goal is to narrow to two or three strong candidates worth developing further.

docs.title: Project Management
docs.text:
Project management is the discipline of planning, organizing, and controlling the work required to complete a defined goal within a given time and budget. For your practicum, project management is not optional — without it, well-intentioned projects stall, run over budget, and miss deadlines.

The five core functions of project management are:

  1. Scope definition — specifying exactly what the project will and will not include
  2. Scheduling — breaking the work into tasks and assigning start and end dates
  3. Resource planning — identifying what materials, tools, and time are needed
  4. Risk management — anticipating what could go wrong and planning responses
  5. Communication — keeping stakeholders informed of progress, changes, and decisions

You will build detailed project management artifacts — a work breakdown structure, a Gantt chart, and a budget — in Chapters 5 and 6. Here, the goal is to understand project management as a mindset: engineering success is not just about a good idea; it is about executing that idea systematically.

docs.title: Engineering Ethics and the Code of Ethics
docs.text:
Engineering ethics is the branch of applied ethics that examines the moral principles and professional values that should guide engineers in their work. Because engineers design systems and structures that affect public safety, the environment, and the quality of daily life, the ethical stakes of engineering decisions are unusually high.

Most major engineering professional societies publish a formal Code of Ethics — a written set of principles that members are expected to uphold. While the specific language varies by organization (NSPE, ASCE, IEEE, ASME all publish their own), every engineering code of ethics shares a common core:

  • Hold the safety, health, and welfare of the public as the paramount concern
  • Be honest and impartial in professional reports, statements, and testimony
  • Act with competence — only undertake work within your area of expertise
  • Avoid conflicts of interest or disclose them fully when they cannot be avoided
  • Protect confidential information entrusted by clients or employers

These codes are not merely aspirational. Licensed Professional Engineers who violate them can face suspension or revocation of their license by the state engineering board.
