docs.title: Professional Responsibility and Public Safety
docs.text:
Professional responsibility refers to an engineer's obligation to perform their work competently, honestly, and with regard for the consequences of their designs on others. This responsibility is especially weighty because engineers often design systems that members of the public use without any ability to evaluate their safety independently.

Public safety in engineering is the principle that no commercial, competitive, or personal interest justifies knowingly placing the public at risk. Engineering history contains sobering examples of what happens when this principle is violated or ignored:

  • The Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940) — resonance effects that engineers understood theoretically were not adequately tested at scale.
  • The Challenger Space Shuttle disaster (1986) — engineers raised O-ring concerns before launch; organizational pressure contributed to the decision to proceed.
  • The Flint, Michigan water crisis (2014–2019) — cost-cutting decisions led to lead contamination of the public water supply, affecting thousands of children.

These cases are studied not to assign blame decades later, but because they reveal the conditions under which ethical failures occur — and the systematic approaches that can prevent them.

docs.title: Ethical Decision Making
docs.text:
Ethical decision making in engineering is the process of recognizing an ethical dimension in a situation, identifying the stakeholders and values involved, evaluating available options against ethical principles, and choosing a course of action that can be professionally defended.

A widely used framework for engineering ethics cases is the following four-step process:

  1. Recognize the ethical issue — Is there a potential for harm? Whose interests are in conflict?
  2. Gather the facts — What do you know? What do you need to find out? Who has relevant expertise?
  3. Evaluate the options — Which option best upholds the core engineering values? What are the consequences of each option for each stakeholder?
  4. Act and reflect — Choose the most defensible option, document your reasoning, and reflect on the outcome.

docs.title: Personal vs. Professional Ethics
docs.text:
A distinction that surprises many students is that personal ethics and professional ethics are not always the same. Personal ethics refers to the values and moral principles a person holds as an individual — shaped by family, culture, religion, and experience. Professional ethics refers to the standards of conduct defined by the profession itself, independent of any individual's personal beliefs.

This distinction matters in engineering because you may encounter situations where your personal preferences differ from what your professional code requires:

  • Personal belief: "I trust my client's word." Professional obligation: An engineer must verify safety-critical claims independently.
  • Personal preference: "I don't want to make trouble by raising this concern." Professional obligation: If you observe a safety hazard, you are obligated to report it.

The professional code is not a replacement for personal ethics — it is a floor, a minimum standard that all engineers must meet regardless of their individual values. Most engineers find that their personal and professional ethics align closely, but when they conflict, the professional code governs professional conduct.

docs.title: Conflict of Interest
docs.text:
A conflict of interest exists when an engineer's personal, financial, or other interests could compromise — or appear to compromise — their professional judgment. Conflicts of interest are among the most common ethical challenges in engineering practice.

Examples of conflicts of interest in engineering:

  • An engineer who owns stock in a materials supplier recommends that supplier's products on a client project without disclosing the ownership.
  • An engineer reviewing a competitor's design for a government contract has a personal friendship with the competitor's lead engineer.
  • An engineer accepts a gift from a vendor who wants to be included in an upcoming project specification.

The standard response to a conflict of interest is disclosure — informing all affected parties and, if the conflict cannot be adequately managed, recusing yourself from the decision. The NSPE Code of Ethics states that engineers shall "act in such a manner as to uphold and enhance the honor, integrity, and dignity of the engineering profession" — which requires proactively managing conflicts even when disclosure is uncomfortable.

docs.title: What Is Intellectual Property?
docs.text:
Intellectual property (IP) refers to creations of the mind — inventions, designs, artistic works, symbols, names, and trade information — that are protected by law. The purpose of IP law is to encourage innovation by giving creators exclusive rights to benefit from their work for a defined period, while ultimately releasing the work into the public domain for everyone's benefit.

For engineers, IP is not an abstract legal concept — it is a practical reality that shapes what you can design, what you can document publicly, and what protections you can seek for your own innovations. Understanding the four main categories of IP protection is essential before you begin designing and documenting your practicum project.

docs.title: Patents
docs.text:
A patent is a legal right granted by the federal government (via the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office, USPTO) that gives the inventor the exclusive right to make, use, and sell an invention for a limited period — typically 20 years from the filing date. In exchange for this exclusive right, the inventor must publicly disclose exactly how the invention works.

The three types of patents most relevant to engineering are:

  • Utility patents — protect new and useful processes, machines, articles of manufacture, or compositions of matter (the most common type for engineering inventions)
  • Design patents — protect the ornamental or aesthetic design of a functional item (14 years)
  • Plant patents — protect distinct and new plant varieties (less common in engineering)

For an invention to be patentable, it must be novel (not previously disclosed), non-obvious (not an obvious improvement to someone skilled in the field), and useful. The patent application process is complex and typically requires a registered patent attorney or agent.

docs.title: Copyrights
docs.text:
A copyright is a form of IP protection that automatically applies to original creative works the moment they are created and fixed in a tangible form — no registration required, though registration with the U.S. Copyright Office provides legal advantages. Copyright protects the expression of an idea, not the idea itself.

In engineering contexts, copyright protects:

  • Technical reports, white papers, and written documentation
  • Engineering drawings and schematics
  • Software source code
  • Photographs and illustrations used in project materials

Copyright lasts for the life of the author plus 70 years (for individual authors). For corporate or "work for hire" authorship, the term is 95 years from publication or 120 years from creation, whichever is shorter.

Important distinction: Copyright does not protect the underlying engineering concepts or methods described in a document. To protect an invention itself, a patent is required.

docs.title: Trademarks
docs.text:
A trademark is a word, phrase, logo, symbol, or combination thereof that identifies the source of a product or service and distinguishes it from competitors. Trademarks are primarily relevant to commercial engineering products rather than engineering processes.

Examples of trademarks in engineering-adjacent contexts:

  • The LEGO® name and brick design
  • The Intel® logo and the "Intel Inside" phrase
  • The caterpillar tracks design associated with Caterpillar Inc.

Trademark rights can last indefinitely as long as the mark remains in use and is actively enforced. Marks registered with the USPTO may use the ® symbol; unregistered marks that have been used in commerce may use the ™ symbol.

docs.title: Trade Secrets
docs.text:
A trade secret is confidential business information that provides a competitive advantage and is protected by keeping it secret — there is no registration process, and protection lasts as long as the information remains confidential. The Coca-Cola formula and Google's search algorithm are famous examples.

In engineering practice, trade secrets commonly include:

  • Proprietary manufacturing processes
  • Customer lists and pricing strategies
  • Internal engineering specifications not disclosed in patents
  • Software algorithms and source code (when kept confidential rather than patented or copyrighted)

The legal protection for trade secrets requires that the owner take "reasonable measures" to keep the information secret — such as non-disclosure agreements (NDAs), restricted access to documents, and secure storage of digital files.

docs.title: IP Protection and Enforcement
docs.text:
Obtaining IP protection is one step; enforcing that protection is another. IP protection and enforcement refers to the legal mechanisms and actions available to the holder of an IP right when that right is violated.

Key enforcement mechanisms include:

  • Civil litigation — filing a lawsuit in federal court seeking an injunction (stop order) and monetary damages
  • Cease and desist letters — a written demand to stop infringing activity, often the first step before litigation
  • International Trade Commission (ITC) proceedings — used to block importation of infringing goods at the U.S. border
  • DMCA takedowns — used to remove infringing digital content under the Digital Millennium Copyright Act

Enforcement is expensive and time-consuming, which is why large corporations employ legal departments dedicated to IP matters. For individual inventors and small engineering firms, the cost of enforcement can sometimes exceed the value of the protected innovation — a practical reality that influences how engineers and companies decide what to protect and how.

docs.title: IP Infringement
docs.text:
IP infringement occurs when someone makes, uses, sells, or distributes protected IP without permission from the rights holder. The consequences of infringement can include civil damages (often calculated as the infringer's profits or the rights holder's lost profits), attorney's fees, and in cases involving willful infringement or trade secret theft, criminal penalties.

For student engineers, the most common IP risk areas are:

  • Using patented components or processes in a design without a license
  • Copying software libraries or code without complying with the applicable open-source license
  • Reproducing images, diagrams, or text from published sources without attribution or permission (copyright infringement)

Fair use is a legal doctrine that allows limited use of copyrighted material without permission — for purposes such as education, commentary, criticism, or research. However, fair use is a defense evaluated case-by-case, not a blanket exemption. When in doubt about whether a use is permissible, cite the source, seek permission, or find an openly licensed alternative.

IP Violation Example Potential Consequence Patent infringement Selling a device that uses a patented mechanism without a license Civil lawsuit; injunction; damages Copyright infringement Reproducing a textbook diagram in a report without permission DMCA takedown; civil lawsuit Trademark infringement Using a competitor's logo on your product Injunction; reputational damage Trade secret theft Sharing an employer's proprietary process with a competitor Criminal charges; civil lawsuit

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
This chapter covered the foundational work of starting an engineering project responsibly. Here is what you should be able to explain and apply:

Defining the Problem:

  • Engineers begin with careful problem identification — not every frustration is a viable engineering problem, but those with measurable impact and a definable scope are.
  • A needs assessment confirms the problem is real and helps you understand it at the root cause level.
  • Opportunity recognition reframes problems as specific, actionable design opportunities with named beneficiaries.
  • An engineering challenge definition captures the situation, need, measurable impact, and goal in a single focused statement.
  • Constraints (non-negotiable limits) and criteria (desired qualities) define the design space before brainstorming begins.
  • Brainstorming uses deferred judgment to generate many ideas before evaluating any of them.
  • Project management applies throughout: scope, schedule, resources, risk, and communication are all managed from day one.

Engineering Ethics:

  • Engineering codes of ethics universally place public safety and welfare as the paramount obligation.
  • Professional responsibility means taking ownership of the safety, accuracy, and consequences of your work.
  • Ethical decision making is a four-step process: recognize, gather facts, evaluate options, act and reflect.
  • Professional ethics are a minimum floor that governs conduct regardless of personal preferences.
  • Conflicts of interest must be disclosed promptly and managed to preserve professional integrity.

Intellectual Property:

  • Patents protect inventions for 20 years in exchange for public disclosure.
  • Copyrights automatically protect original creative expression from the moment of creation.
  • Trademarks protect brand identifiers that distinguish products in the marketplace.
  • Trade secrets protect confidential information through secrecy rather than registration.
  • IP infringement carries civil and sometimes criminal consequences — understanding what you may and may not use is a professional obligation.

All of these concepts apply directly to your practicum project. Your problem statement is due as the first deliverable. Make it specific, measurable, and grounded in a real need.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Before committing to a design direction, engineers must evaluate whether their solution is safe and identify what could go wrong. This chapter teaches students to apply safety standards to their designs, systematically identify risks, analyze their likelihood and impact, and develop strategies to reduce or eliminate them — a critical step before the project proposal is written.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text: Risk Management — CTE-51, CTE-62

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 5 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Safety Standards
  2. Design Safety Evaluation
  3. Risk Identification
  4. Risk Analysis
  5. Risk Mitigation Strategies

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 2: Engineering Foundations and Industry Examination
  • Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Every engineering design carries some degree of risk. A bridge might fail under an unexpected load. A medical device might malfunction at a critical moment. A software system might leak private data. Engineers do not eliminate risk entirely — that is often impossible — but they are professionally and ethically obligated to identify risks, understand them, and reduce them to an acceptable level before any design is built or deployed.

Risk management is not a bureaucratic formality. It is one of the most direct expressions of the engineering code of ethics you studied in Chapter 4: placing the safety, health, and welfare of the public above all other considerations. This chapter gives you the tools to do that work systematically for your own practicum project.

docs.title: Safety Standards
docs.text:
A safety standard is a documented set of requirements, guidelines, or specifications that defines acceptable performance for an engineering design in a given context. Safety standards are developed by government agencies, professional engineering organizations, and industry bodies — not individual engineers — precisely because safety decisions should not depend on one person's judgment alone.

Before you can evaluate your design against a standard, you need to know which standards apply to your project. The governing standard depends on the domain your project touches:

Domain Example Standards Body Example Standard Electrical systems UL (Underwriters Laboratories), IEEE UL 62368-1 (audio/video equipment) Structural / civil ASCE (American Society of Civil Engineers) ASCE 7 (minimum design loads) Mechanical / pressure ASME (American Society of Mechanical Engineers) ASME B31.3 (process piping) Consumer products CPSC (Consumer Product Safety Commission) 16 CFR Part 1500 (hazardous substances) Environmental EPA National Ambient Air Quality Standards Medical devices FDA, ISO ISO 13485 (quality management for medical devices)

For high school practicum projects, your design will rarely need to comply with a formal regulatory standard — but understanding which standard would apply in a real deployment helps you think like a professional engineer.

docs.title: Design Safety Evaluation
docs.text:
Design safety evaluation is the process of systematically comparing a proposed engineering design against relevant safety standards and criteria to determine whether the design is acceptable before resources are committed to building it. Catching a safety problem at the design stage costs far less — in time, money, and potentially human harm — than discovering it after construction or deployment.

The core technique is a structured checklist review. For each component or function of your design, you ask:

  • Does this component meet the applicable safety standard?
  • What happens if this component fails? Does failure cascade to other parts?
  • Are there guards, interlocks, or redundancies that prevent or contain failure?
  • Are the materials, tolerances, and energy levels within accepted safe ranges?

Two of the most widely used formal safety evaluation methods in professional engineering are FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) and FTA (Fault Tree Analysis). FMEA works bottom-up: you list every component, identify every way it could fail, and trace the effects of that failure through the system. FTA works top-down: you start with a catastrophic outcome you want to prevent and work backward to find combinations of events that could cause it. Both methods are more rigorous than a simple checklist, and both appear in engineering practice in regulated industries like aerospace, automotive, and medical devices.

For your practicum project, a structured design safety checklist — rather than a full FMEA — is the appropriate tool. Your checklist should address materials safety, energy hazards, user interaction points, failure modes for critical components, and environmental impact.

docs.title: Diagram: FMEA Process Walkthrough
docs.text:
Interactive FMEA Step-by-Step Walkthrough

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: fmea-walkthrough Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) the FMEA process by stepping through each stage of a simplified Failure Mode and Effects Analysis for a sample engineering component.

Canvas: 800 × 480 px, responsive to window resize.

Interaction model: A five-step progress bar at the top labels the stages: (1) List Components, (2) Identify Failure Modes, (3) Determine Effects, (4) Rate Severity × Occurrence, (5) Identify Controls. The student clicks "Next Step" to advance through each stage. At each stage, the main panel shows: - A description of what to do at this stage (2–3 sentences) - An example row from a sample project (e.g., a motorized gate latch) - An editable text field where the student can type their own component or failure mode (for reflection, not graded)

Stage 4 detail: Displays two sliders — Severity (1–10) and Occurrence (1–10) — and calculates a Risk Priority Number (RPN = Severity × Occurrence × Detectability, where Detectability is a third slider). The RPN updates in real time. Color coding: RPN < 50 = green, 50–100 = yellow, > 100 = red.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/fmea-walkthrough/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 540 px height.

docs.title: Risk Identification
docs.text:
Before you can manage a risk, you must find it. Risk identification is the process of systematically discovering, naming, and documenting every potential event or condition that could negatively affect your project's outcome, timeline, budget, or safety. The goal is not to predict the future with certainty but to ensure that no significant threat goes unexamined.

Effective risk identification draws on several techniques used together:

  • Brainstorming — Free, structured group discussion focused on "what could go wrong?" with no idea dismissed too early.
  • Assumption analysis — Reviewing every assumption your project plan makes (e.g., "the materials will arrive on time," "the motor will operate at the rated voltage") and asking what happens if that assumption is wrong.
  • Checklist review — Using a pre-built category list to prompt thinking in areas that brainstorming might miss.
  • Expert interviews — Asking someone with relevant experience what problems they have seen in similar projects.

For a practicum project, four broad risk categories cover most threats you will encounter:

Risk Category Examples Technical risks Design flaw discovered late, material behaves unexpectedly, software bug Schedule risks Supplier delay, school closure, unexpected complexity Resource risks Budget overrun, tool unavailable, needed expertise missing Safety risks Electrical hazard, sharp edge, chemical exposure, structural failure

Every identified risk should be recorded in a risk register — a simple table that names the risk, describes its potential cause, and tracks its status throughout the project. The risk register becomes the working document you update as the project progresses.

docs.title: Risk Analysis
docs.text:
Finding a risk is not enough — you also need to understand how serious it is. Risk analysis is the process of evaluating each identified risk on two dimensions: likelihood (how probable is it that this risk will occur?) and impact (how severe would the consequences be if it did occur?). Together, these two dimensions produce a risk score that tells you where to focus your attention.

Both likelihood and impact are rated on a consistent scale. A common scale used in engineering practice assigns values from 1 to 5:

Rating Likelihood Impact 1 Rare — unlikely under normal conditions Negligible — minor inconvenience, easily corrected 2 Unlikely — possible but not expected Minor — small delay or cost increase 3 Possible — may occur in some circumstances Moderate — noticeable effect on schedule, budget, or quality 4 Likely — expected to occur at some point Significant — major rework required or safety concern 5 Almost certain — will probably occur Catastrophic — project failure or serious harm

The risk score is the product of Likelihood × Impact. A risk rated Likelihood 4, Impact 3 has a score of 12, which is higher priority than a risk rated Likelihood 2, Impact 5 (score of 10) — even though the second risk would be more severe if it occurred.

The tool that visualizes all of this at once is the risk matrix.

docs.title: Diagram: Interactive Risk Matrix
docs.text:
Interactive Risk Matrix — Likelihood × Impact

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: risk-matrix Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will analyze (Bloom L4) a set of project risks by placing them on a likelihood-impact grid and observing how risk scores determine priority.

Canvas: 700 × 600 px, responsive.

Grid: A 5 × 5 matrix. The X-axis is Impact (1–5, left to right). The Y-axis is Likelihood (1–5, bottom to top). Each cell is color-coded by zone: - Green (score 1–4): Acceptable risk — monitor only - Yellow (score 5–9): Moderate risk — plan a response - Orange (score 10–15): High risk — active mitigation required - Red (score 16–25): Critical risk — immediate action, consider redesign

Risk tokens: Six pre-populated sample risk tokens appear as labeled circles on the grid (e.g., "Motor fails," "Budget overrun," "Material delay"). The student can drag each token to a new cell to re-rate it.

Hover behavior: Hovering over any cell shows a tooltip: "Score: [value] — [zone label and recommended response]." Hovering over a token shows its full risk description (1–2 sentences).

Add risk button: A small form below the grid lets the student type a new risk name and place it on the grid with a click.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/risk-matrix/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 660 px height.

Once all risks are plotted on the matrix, you can read your priorities clearly: anything in the red or orange zone demands a response before the project moves forward. Yellow risks should be planned for. Green risks are documented and monitored but do not require immediate action.

docs.title: Risk Mitigation Strategies
docs.text:
Knowing that a risk is high-priority tells you it needs attention — but not what to do about it. Risk mitigation strategies are the specific actions a project team takes to reduce the likelihood or impact of an identified risk, or to prepare for a fast recovery if the risk does occur.

There are four fundamental strategies, and choosing the right one depends on the risk's nature, its score, and the resources available:

  • Avoid — Change the project plan to eliminate the risk entirely. Example: if using a high-voltage component creates a critical electrical hazard, redesign to use low-voltage alternatives.
  • Reduce — Take action to lower the likelihood or impact. Example: order materials two weeks early to reduce the impact of a supplier delay.
  • Transfer — Shift the consequence to another party. Example: purchase materials insurance, or use a vendor who guarantees replacement of defective parts.
  • Accept — Acknowledge the risk and decide to proceed without additional action, typically for low-scoring risks. Acceptance should always be a conscious, documented choice — not just an oversight.

Most practicum projects will rely primarily on avoid and reduce strategies, because transfer options require contracts and financial resources that students typically do not control.

For every high-priority risk (orange or red on your matrix), your risk management plan should document:

  1. The risk name and description
  2. Its likelihood and impact ratings
  3. The chosen mitigation strategy (avoid / reduce / transfer / accept)
  4. Specific action steps and who is responsible for each
  5. A contingency plan — what you will do if the risk occurs despite mitigation

docs.title: Diagram: Risk Response Strategy Selector
docs.text:
Risk Response Strategy Decision Tool

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: risk-response-selector Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will evaluate (Bloom L5) which risk response strategy is most appropriate for a given risk scenario by working through a structured decision guide.

Canvas: 750 × 450 px, responsive.

Interaction model: A two-axis slider panel on the left allows the student to set a risk's Likelihood (1–5) and Impact (1–5). Based on these values, the right panel displays: - The risk score (L × I) - The risk zone (green / yellow / orange / red) - A ranked recommendation of the four strategies (Avoid, Reduce, Transfer, Accept) with a one-sentence rationale for each, highlighted by appropriateness for the current score.

When the student clicks on a strategy name, a panel below expands to show: what the strategy means, an example in an engineering context, and one question to ask when writing the action steps.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/risk-response-selector/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 520 px height.

docs.title: Putting It Together: Your Risk Management Plan
docs.text:
Your Risk Management assignment asks you to apply these concepts directly to your practicum project. The deliverable is a completed risk register that covers at least six identified risks, with full analysis and mitigation strategies for any risk scoring 8 or higher.

Use this sequence:

  1. Identify at least ten candidate risks across all four categories (technical, schedule, resource, safety).
  2. Rate each risk on the 1–5 likelihood and impact scales.
  3. Calculate the risk score and place each risk on the matrix.
  4. Select a mitigation strategy for every risk scoring 8 or higher.
  5. Document specific action steps and a contingency plan for each high-priority risk.
  6. Review the safety-specific risks with your teacher before finalizing your design direction.

A well-prepared risk management plan does more than satisfy the assignment — it makes your proposal stronger, because a peer reviewer or judge can see that you have thought critically about what could go wrong and have a credible plan to address it.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
Risk management is the discipline of finding problems before they find you. Here is what you should be able to explain after completing this chapter:

  • Safety standards are external, authoritative documents that define acceptable performance levels; they are not optional suggestions.
  • Design safety evaluation means checking your design against those standards before committing to building it — FMEA and FTA are the professional methods; a structured checklist is the appropriate starting point for this course.
  • Risk identification draws on brainstorming, assumption analysis, checklists, and expert input to surface threats across four categories: technical, schedule, resource, and safety.
  • Risk analysis scores each risk on Likelihood × Impact and visualizes the results on a risk matrix to set response priorities.
  • Risk mitigation strategies — avoid, reduce, transfer, or accept — translate your analysis into specific, assigned action steps.

Your Risk Management assignment puts all five concepts into practice for your specific project. Complete it before your project proposal is written — your proposal will be stronger for it.

docs.title: Chapter 6 - Project Work Schedule and Budget
Chapter 6: Project Work Schedule and Budget
Summary
docs.text: With a problem defined and risks assessed, students now plan the execution of their practicum project in detail. This chapter covers the full project planning toolkit — breaking work into manageable pieces, building a realistic schedule with industry-standard tools, and developing a budget grounded in real cost research — so students can demonstrate to stakeholders that their solution is achievable within the course timeframe.

docs.title: Assignments
docs.text:
• Project Work Schedule — CTE-55, CTE-59
  • Budget — CTE-56, CTE-58

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 12 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Milestone Planning
  2. Schedule Development
  3. Scheduling Tools
  4. Gantt Chart
  5. Work Schedule Creation
  6. Work Breakdown Structure
  7. Project Management Software
  8. Resource Allocation
  9. Budget Research Methods
  10. Cost Estimation
  11. Cost-Benefit Analysis
  12. Budget Development

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 1: Engineering Field Exploration
  • Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
A well-designed solution that cannot be built on time or within budget is not a viable engineering solution. Professional engineers do not simply start building after a problem is defined — they invest significant effort in planning the work before the work begins. That planning produces two foundational documents: a project schedule and a project budget.

In this chapter you will learn how working engineers break large projects into trackable pieces, sequence those pieces logically, assign time estimates, and translate the resulting plan into a visual schedule. You will then learn how to research real costs, apply estimation techniques, and evaluate whether a project is worth pursuing financially. Both documents you produce here will become key sections of your project proposal in Chapter 7.

docs.title: The Work Breakdown Structure
docs.text:
Before you can schedule anything, you need to know exactly what needs to be done. A Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) is a hierarchical decomposition of all the work required to complete a project. It breaks the project down from a single goal at the top into progressively smaller deliverables and tasks at lower levels.

The WBS uses a tree structure with three typical levels:

  • Level 1 — Project (the overall goal, e.g., "Automated Plant Watering System")
  • Level 2 — Major Deliverables (e.g., "Hardware Assembly," "Software Development," "Testing and Documentation")
  • Level 3 — Work Packages (specific, assignable tasks, e.g., "Wire moisture sensor to microcontroller," "Write data-logging function," "Conduct three-day field test")

A work package is the fundamental unit of the WBS. It should be small enough to estimate accurately (typically 4–20 hours of effort) and produce a concrete, verifiable output.

docs.title: Diagram: WBS Builder
docs.text:
Interactive Work Breakdown Structure Builder

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: wbs-builder Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will construct (Bloom L6 — Create) a three-level WBS for their practicum project by typing deliverables and tasks into an editable tree structure.

Canvas: 900 × 540 px, responsive to window resize.

Layout: Tree drawn top-down. The root node (Level 1) is centered at the top. Level 2 nodes branch below it. Level 3 nodes branch below each Level 2 node. Nodes are rounded rectangles; lines connect parent to children.

Controls: - "Add Deliverable" button — appends a new Level 2 node to the tree (labeled "Deliverable N") - "Add Task" button — appends a Level 3 node under the currently selected Level 2 node - Double-click any node to enter edit mode (inline text field); press Enter or click away to confirm - Click a node to select it (highlighted with a gold border) - Right-click a node → context menu with "Delete" option

Hover behavior: Hovering over any node shows a tooltip: "Level 2 = Major Deliverable (a grouping of related tasks). Level 3 = Work Package (one specific, measurable task)."

Export: A "Copy as Text" button formats the tree as an indented outline and copies it to the clipboard, which students can paste into their planning document.

Implementation: Standalone HTML at docs/sims/wbs-builder/index.html. Iframe in chapter at 580 px height.

docs.title: Milestone Planning
docs.text:
Once the WBS is built, the next step is to identify milestones. A milestone is a significant event or checkpoint in a project that marks the completion of a major phase or deliverable. Milestones do not represent work — they represent achievement. Saying "Hardware assembled and verified" is a milestone; "Solder components to circuit board" is a task.

Milestones serve several purposes in project planning:

  • They give the team short-term targets that are easier to stay motivated around than a distant final deadline
  • They give stakeholders (your teacher, your review panel) visible checkpoints to assess progress
  • They serve as decision gates — if a milestone is missed or delivered with problems, the team evaluates whether to continue, revise the plan, or change direction

For a practicum project lasting one semester, you should identify approximately four to eight milestones, spaced roughly three to four weeks apart.

docs.title: Schedule Development
docs.text:
Schedule development is the process of assigning start dates, end dates, and durations to each task in the WBS — then sequencing those tasks logically based on dependencies. A task dependency is a relationship between two tasks that constrains when one can start relative to when another finishes.

There are four standard dependency types:

Dependency Type Meaning Example Finish-to-Start (FS) B cannot start until A finishes Write code → Test code Start-to-Start (SS) B cannot start until A starts Research A and Research B can run in parallel Finish-to-Finish (FF) B cannot finish until A finishes Report editing finishes when data collection finishes Start-to-Finish (SF) B cannot finish until A starts Rare; mostly used in shift-handoff planning

Finish-to-Start is by far the most common dependency type you will use. Identifying your FS dependencies first will reveal the critical path — the longest chain of dependent tasks from start to finish. Any delay on the critical path delays the entire project.

docs.title: The Gantt Chart
docs.text:
A Gantt chart is a horizontal bar chart that displays the schedule for a project, with tasks listed on the vertical axis, time on the horizontal axis, and bars that show the duration and timing of each task. Named after mechanical engineer Henry Gantt (1861–1919), who popularized them in early 20th-century manufacturing, Gantt charts remain the most widely used schedule visualization in engineering and project management today.

Key elements of a Gantt chart include:

  • Task bars — horizontal bars whose length represents task duration
  • Milestone diamonds — diamond shapes marking key completion points
  • Dependency arrows — lines connecting tasks that have finish-to-start relationships
  • Today line — a vertical line showing the current date, used during execution to compare planned vs. actual progress

docs.title: Diagram: Interactive Gantt Chart Builder
docs.text:
Interactive Gantt Chart Builder MicroSim

Type: MicroSim sim-id: gantt-chart-builder Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) schedule development skills by building a Gantt chart from a task list with durations and dependencies, and observing how changing one task's duration shifts the critical path.

Canvas: Full-width × 560 px, responsive.

Left panel (280 px) — Task Input Table: A table with four columns: Task Name (text input), Start Week (number 1–16), Duration (weeks, number 1–8), and Is Milestone? (checkbox). An "Add Task" button adds a new row. A "Clear All" button resets to a starter set of 5 sample tasks.

Right panel — Gantt View: Horizontal axis: weeks 1–16 (representing a 16-week semester). Vertical axis: task rows matching left-panel order. Each task is drawn as a blue bar; milestone tasks are rendered as gold diamonds at their start week. A red vertical line marks "Week 6" (the current week by default — adjustable via a slider labeled "Today").

Critical Path Highlighting: A "Show Critical Path" toggle highlights in red all task bars that form the longest dependent chain. Dependency connections are specified by the student entering a comma-separated list of prerequisite task numbers in an optional "Depends On" column.

Hover behavior: Hovering over any bar shows a tooltip: task name, start date, end date, and whether it is on the critical path.

Export: A "Print View" button opens a print-optimized version of the Gantt chart in a new tab.

Implementation: Standalone HTML at docs/sims/gantt-chart-builder/index.html. Iframe at 620 px height.

docs.title: Scheduling Tools and Project Management Software
docs.text:
Professional engineers rarely build Gantt charts by hand. They use scheduling tools — software applications designed to create, maintain, and share project schedules. Understanding the tools available helps you choose the right one for your project's complexity and your team's resources.

Tool Type Cost Best For Microsoft Project Desktop + cloud Paid (often school-licensed) Complex, multi-resource projects Smartsheet Cloud Freemium Team collaboration and real-time updates Asana Cloud Free tier available Task tracking with Gantt add-on Trello Cloud Free tier available Kanban-style task boards Google Sheets Cloud Free Simple Gantt charts built manually ProjectLibre Desktop Free, open-source MS Project alternative

Project management software is the broader category that includes scheduling tools but also covers task assignment, file sharing, budget tracking, and communication. For your practicum project, any tool that lets you display tasks, durations, and dates in a Gantt-style view is sufficient.

docs.title: Work Schedule Creation and Resource Allocation
docs.text:
A work schedule is the final, committed version of your project plan — the document you will actually use to guide your work and measure your progress against. It combines your WBS, your Gantt chart, and your resource assignments into one living document.

Resource allocation is the process of assigning the people, materials, equipment, and time needed to complete each task. For a solo practicum project, the primary resource is your own time. Allocating it realistically means estimating not just how long each task takes, but when during the week you have time to work on it.

To build a realistic work schedule:

  1. List all tasks from your WBS with durations
  2. Assign each task to a week range in the semester
  3. Check that no week exceeds your available work hours (typically 3–5 hours per week for this course)
  4. Identify your milestones and mark their target dates
  5. Build in at least one buffer week before major deadlines — unexpected delays are common

docs.title: Budget Research Methods
docs.text:
A project budget is only as good as the data behind it. Before you can estimate what anything will cost, you need to research real prices and real options. Budget research methods are the strategies engineers use to gather accurate cost data for materials, labor, equipment, and services.

The most reliable budget research strategies for a high school practicum project are:

  • Supplier catalogs and online stores — browse Adafruit, SparkFun, Home Depot, McMaster-Carr, Grainger, or Amazon to find real current prices for materials and components
  • Requesting quotes — for custom fabrication or specialized services, email suppliers and ask for a formal price quote
  • Benchmarking — find similar existing products and use their retail price as a reference for what your solution's components should cost
  • Expert interviews — ask engineers, fabricators, or teachers with field experience whether your estimates seem realistic
  • Historical data — if a similar project was done at your school in a prior year, its budget can serve as a starting point

The key principle is to use real numbers from real sources, never guesses. Document every cost source with a URL or supplier name, because your proposal reviewers will want to verify that your budget is credible.

docs.title: Cost Estimation
docs.text:
Cost estimation is the process of calculating the expected total cost of a project based on your research. It translates a list of materials, tools, and services into line-item dollar amounts and totals them.

A well-structured cost estimate for a practicum project includes:

  • Direct materials — components, raw materials, and consumables you will purchase specifically for this project
  • Direct labor — your time, valued at a reasonable hourly rate (for a school project this is often $0, but professional budgets always include labor)
  • Equipment — tools or machinery you need, whether purchased, borrowed, or rented
  • Services — printing, fabrication, or testing performed by an outside vendor
  • Contingency — an additional 10–15% of the total budget set aside for unexpected costs

Contingency is not a guess — it is a deliberate financial buffer that every professional engineering budget includes. Skipping contingency is one of the most common reasons student budgets fail in practice.

docs.title: Diagram: Budget Estimation Worksheet
docs.text:
Interactive Budget Line-Item Calculator

Type: MicroSim sim-id: budget-calculator Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) cost estimation by entering line items into a budget worksheet and observing how subtotals and contingency amounts update in real time.

Canvas: 860 × 520 px, responsive.

Layout: A spreadsheet-style table with the following columns: Category (dropdown: Materials / Equipment / Services / Labor / Other), Item Description (text input), Quantity (number), Unit Cost $ (number), Total (auto-calculated), Source/URL (text input).

Controls: - "Add Row" button adds a new line item - "Remove" button on each row removes it - "Set Contingency %" slider (5%–25%, default 10%)

Summary panel (right side, 200 px wide): - Materials subtotal - Equipment subtotal - Services subtotal - Labor subtotal - Other subtotal - Subtotal - Contingency amount (= subtotal × contingency %) - Grand Total (bold, larger font)

All totals update instantly when any input changes.

Hover on "Grand Total": Tooltip reads "This is your total estimated project cost including contingency. Present this number in your proposal."

Export: "Copy as Markdown Table" button formats the full budget as a markdown table for pasting into the project proposal document.

Implementation: Standalone HTML at docs/sims/budget-calculator/index.html. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Cost-Benefit Analysis
docs.text:
Knowing what a project will cost is only half of the financial picture. The other half is understanding what the project is worth — what problems it solves, what value it creates, and whether that value justifies the cost. This evaluation is called a cost-benefit analysis (CBA).

A cost-benefit analysis is a systematic comparison of the total expected costs of a project against the total expected benefits. In professional engineering, CBA is used to decide whether to fund a project, choose between competing solutions, or justify expenditure to stakeholders.

Benefits are not always expressed in dollars. For an engineering practicum project, benefits may include:

  • Quantitative benefits — time saved per week, money saved per year, energy reduced per month (these can be converted to dollar equivalents)
  • Qualitative benefits — improved safety, reduced user frustration, environmental impact, community value (these are described narratively)

A simple CBA format for your proposal:

Item Details Value Total Project Cost From your budget estimate $XXX Benefit 1 (quantitative) e.g., "Saves 30 min/day × 180 days" $YYY equivalent Benefit 2 (qualitative) e.g., "Reduces risk of plant loss" Narrative Break-even point When cumulative savings exceed project cost X weeks/months Net benefit over 1 year Total benefits − Total cost $ZZZ or N/A

For school projects with budgets under $100, a dollar-for-dollar ROI may not be the main point. What matters is that you can articulate clearly what problem your solution solves and why the cost is justified.

docs.title: Budget Development
docs.text:
Budget development is the final step — assembling all of your research and estimates into a formal, presented budget document. A project budget is not a rough sketch; it is a professional document that communicates your financial planning to reviewers.

A complete project budget for your practicum includes four elements:

  1. Line-item cost table — every material, tool, and service, with quantities, unit costs, sources, and totals
  2. Contingency line — 10–15% of the subtotal, labeled clearly
  3. Grand total — the amount you are requesting or committing to spend
  4. Cost-benefit summary — a brief statement of what the project delivers in return for the cost

docs.title: Diagram: Cost-Benefit Decision Explorer
docs.text:
Cost-Benefit Scenario Explorer

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: cost-benefit-explorer Library: Chart.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will evaluate (Bloom L5) how changing project costs or benefit assumptions shifts the break-even point, developing intuition for financial trade-offs in engineering decisions.

Canvas: 820 × 460 px, responsive.

Controls (left, 260 px): - Project Cost slider: $10–$500 (default $80) - Weekly benefit ($ equivalent) slider: $1–$50 (default $5) - Project lifespan slider: 4–52 weeks (default 20)

Visualization (right): A line chart with two series: - "Cumulative Cost" — a flat horizontal line at the project cost value - "Cumulative Benefit" — a rising line starting at $0 and increasing by the weekly benefit amount each week

The intersection of the two lines is highlighted with a gold dot labeled "Break-even at Week N." Beyond the intersection, the benefit line is filled green to indicate positive return. Before the intersection, the gap between lines is filled red.

Hover: Hovering on any point of the benefit line shows a tooltip: "Week N: Total benefit = $X, Net = $Y."

Implementation: Standalone HTML at docs/sims/cost-benefit-explorer/index.html. Iframe at 520 px height.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
Planning is not overhead — it is engineering. The schedule and budget you produce in this chapter are not bureaucratic requirements; they are the evidence that your project is feasible and that you have thought carefully about how to execute it.

Here is what you should be able to explain after completing this chapter:

  • A Work Breakdown Structure decomposes the entire project into trackable work packages before any scheduling begins.
  • Milestone planning creates meaningful checkpoints that give you and your stakeholders short-term targets to track.
  • Schedule development sequences tasks using dependency relationships, identifies the critical path, and assigns realistic durations.
  • A Gantt chart visualizes the full schedule on a time axis with task bars, milestone markers, and dependency lines.
  • Project management software (MS Project, Smartsheet, Google Sheets, etc.) is the standard professional tool for building and maintaining schedules.
  • Resource allocation matches your available time and materials to the tasks in your plan, preventing over-commitment.
  • Budget research methods require real prices from real suppliers — not estimates from memory.
  • Cost estimation adds contingency (10–15%) to every total to account for the unexpected.
  • Cost-benefit analysis answers the question: "Is this project worth doing?"
  • A formal budget document presents all of the above to stakeholders in a credible, verifiable format.

Your Project Work Schedule and Budget assignments will take each of these concepts from this chapter into a real plan for your specific practicum project. Start with the WBS and work outward — everything else flows from knowing exactly what needs to be done.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter brings together everything from the planning phase into a formal engineering proposal. Students learn how to structure and write a compelling proposal, develop the presentation and visual communication skills needed to defend it, and then experience the peer review process from both sides — as presenters and as critical reviewers providing constructive feedback.

docs.title: Assignment
docs.text: Project Proposal Pitch Presentation — CTE-52, CTE-53

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 8 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Presentation Skills
  2. Visual Communication
  3. Proposal Writing
  4. Project Proposal Structure
  5. Proposal Review Criteria
  6. Constructive Feedback
  7. Proposal Defense
  8. Peer Review Process

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
This chapter builds on concepts from:

  • Chapter 4: Problem Statement, Planning, Ethics, and IP
  • Chapter 5: Risk Management
  • Chapter 6: Project Work Schedule and Budget

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
At this point in the course you have defined your engineering problem, assessed its risks, and built a realistic schedule and budget. All of that work now comes together in one document and one presentation: your project proposal. A proposal is the bridge between planning and doing — it is the moment you make the case to stakeholders that your project is worth pursuing.

Professional engineers write proposals constantly. Firms compete for contracts by submitting proposals. Researchers seek funding through proposals. Internal project teams propose new initiatives to leadership. Mastering the proposal format is one of the most transferable skills this course will give you.

This chapter walks you through writing a complete engineering proposal, building a slide deck that communicates your plan clearly, defending that plan under questioning, and participating in a structured peer review of your classmates' proposals.

docs.title: Proposal Writing
docs.text:
Proposal writing is the process of constructing a persuasive, evidence-based document that explains what engineering problem you intend to solve, how you plan to solve it, and why your approach is feasible and worthwhile. Every word in a proposal serves one of three purposes: it describes a problem, justifies an approach, or demonstrates readiness.

A well-written proposal is not a dump of your planning documents. It is a curated, audience-aware argument. You will draw from your problem statement, risk analysis, schedule, and budget — but you will synthesize them into a coherent narrative that a reader can follow without knowing your work history.

Before looking at structure, consider your audience. For this course your audience is your teacher and your classmates. In a professional context it might be a client, a funding agency, or an executive team. The question to ask yourself before writing every section is: What does this reader need to know to trust my plan?

docs.title: Project Proposal Structure
docs.text:
Every strong engineering proposal follows a recognizable structure. The sections below are standard across engineering disciplines, though their names and order may vary slightly by organization or context.

A project proposal structure is the organized sequence of sections that together make the complete case for a project. Think of it as the skeleton — the content you write is the muscle and connective tissue, but without a sound skeleton the whole thing collapses.

The standard sections are:

  1. Title Page — project title, author name(s), date, course or organization name
  2. Executive Summary — a 150–250 word overview of the entire proposal; written last but placed first
  3. Problem Statement — the need or opportunity your project addresses, with supporting evidence
  4. Proposed Solution — your design concept, including constraints, criteria, and how you will meet them
  5. Methodology — the step-by-step plan for how you will execute the project
  6. Risk Analysis — the risks you identified, their likelihood and impact, and your mitigation strategies
  7. Schedule — your project timeline (Gantt chart or equivalent)
  8. Budget — itemized cost estimate with justification
  9. Conclusion — a brief restatement of why the project is feasible and worth doing
  10. References — all sources cited in the proposal, in a consistent citation format

docs.title: Diagram: Interactive Proposal Structure Explorer
docs.text:
Interactive Proposal Section Guide

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: proposal-structure-explorer Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will recall (Bloom L1) the purpose and content of each proposal section by clicking through an interactive document skeleton.

Canvas: 800 × 520 px, responsive to window resize.

Layout: A vertical stack of 10 labeled section tiles (matching the numbered list above). Each tile is a rounded rectangle, color-coded by section category:

  • Administrative (Title, Exec Summary): slate blue
  • Problem/Solution (Problem Statement, Proposed Solution): orange
  • Planning (Methodology, Risk, Schedule, Budget): teal
  • Closure (Conclusion, References): gray-green

Interaction: Clicking any tile expands it to show: (a) The section's purpose in one sentence (b) The typical length (e.g., "1 paragraph", "1 page", "Gantt chart") (c) A "Common mistake" callout in red italics (e.g., "Writing the Executive Summary first — always write it last") (d) A "Pro tip" callout in green italics

Clicking the expanded tile collapses it. Only one tile is expanded at a time.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/proposal-structure-explorer/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Writing the Executive Summary
docs.text:
The executive summary deserves special attention because students often misunderstand its purpose. It is not an introduction and not a table of contents. It is a self-contained mini-proposal that a busy decision-maker could read in place of the full document if pressed for time.

Write the executive summary last, after every other section is complete. Draw one or two key sentences from each major section. The summary should state the problem, the solution concept, the key risk and how you address it, the total budget, and the projected timeline — all in 150–250 words.

A reader who finishes your executive summary should know whether they want to read the full proposal. If your summary is vague or generic, you have not done the job.

docs.title: Presentation Skills
docs.text:
A written proposal gets you in the room. Presentation skills — the techniques and habits that make a speaker credible, clear, and engaging — determine whether you leave that room with approval.

Strong presenters share a set of observable behaviors. Before examining visual communication, internalize these foundational habits:

  • Eye contact — Look at specific people, not the screen or the floor. In a classroom setting, move your eye contact around so every quadrant of the audience gets attention.
  • Pace — Most nervous speakers rush. Record yourself once and count your words per minute. Aim for 120–150 words per minute; slower is usually clearer.
  • Pausing — A deliberate pause after a key point signals importance. Silence is not dead air — it is processing time for your audience.
  • Volume and projection — Speak to the back row, even in a small room. If you are unsure whether you are loud enough, you probably are not.
  • Posture and stillness — Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart. Avoid swaying, pacing, or fidgeting, which all signal anxiety and distract from your message.
  • Handling questions — Repeat the question back before answering. This confirms you understood it and gives you a moment to think.

None of these habits are innate — all of them improve with deliberate practice. The mock rehearsals you do before your pitch are not optional extras; they are where the skills develop.

docs.title: Visual Communication
docs.text:
Visual communication is the use of graphics, layout, color, and typography to convey information more efficiently or memorably than words alone. In an engineering proposal pitch, your slide deck is your primary visual communication tool.

Three principles govern effective slide design:

1. One idea per slide. If a slide requires more than one headline to describe its content, it should be two slides. Crowded slides split attention between reading and listening, and the audience will do neither well.

2. Visuals over bullets. A photograph of your prototype speaks faster than a bullet point describing it. A Gantt chart communicates your schedule in seconds; a paragraph describing the same schedule takes minutes. Default to the visual; use text to label and explain, not to replace the image.

3. Contrast and hierarchy. Your audience scans a slide before they read it. Use size (largest = most important), color contrast (key information stands out from the background), and whitespace (breathing room between elements) to guide their eyes to what matters most.

The table below summarizes common slide mistakes and their fixes.

Common Mistake Why It Fails Fix Wall of bullet points Audience reads instead of listening Replace with an image, chart, or single key statement Fonts smaller than 24pt Back-row audience cannot read it Set a 24pt minimum for body text; 32pt+ for headlines More than 6 slides for a 5-minute pitch Too much content, not enough time One slide per minute is a reliable rule of thumb No slide numbers Hard to reference in Q&A Add slide numbers in the footer of every slide Clip art or stock photos Looks unprofessional and distracting Use your own photos, diagrams, or openly licensed imagery Reading the slides word-for-word You become redundant; audience disengages Slides are visual anchors — you are the speaker, not the script

docs.title: Diagram: Slide Quality Analyzer
docs.text:
Slide Quality Self-Check MicroSim

Type: MicroSim sim-id: slide-quality-analyzer Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will evaluate (Bloom L5) whether a sample slide meets visual communication standards by scoring it against six criteria and receiving automated feedback.

Canvas: 900 × 520 px, responsive.

Layout: Left panel (400 px): Displays a sample slide thumbnail — a fictitious but realistic-looking slide from a student proposal. Three different slides are available via Previous / Next buttons. Each sample slide has deliberately mixed quality (some elements strong, some weak).

Right panel (500 px): A checklist of six criteria, each with a Yes / No / Partial toggle:

  1. Does the slide have one clear main idea?
  2. Is all body text at least 24pt?
  3. Is the visual-to-text ratio at least 60% visual?
  4. Is there strong contrast between text and background?
  5. Does the title accurately describe the slide content?
  6. Would a back-row viewer understand this in 10 seconds?

Below the checklist: a color-coded score bar (0–6) that updates as the user toggles answers. At score 5–6: green "Strong slide." At 3–4: yellow "Needs one fix." At 0–2: red "Redesign this slide."

Below the score bar: A feedback panel that lists specific improvement suggestions matched to the criteria marked No or Partial.

Reset button clears all toggles for the current slide.

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/slide-quality-analyzer/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 580 px height.

docs.title: Proposal Defense
docs.text:
A proposal defense is the question-and-answer session that follows a proposal presentation. It is the moment when stakeholders — clients, reviewers, or in this course your teacher and classmates — test the depth and soundness of your plan by asking probing questions.

Many students find the defense the most stressful part of the pitch. It feels unpredictable because you do not control the questions. However, experienced presenters know that the vast majority of defense questions fall into a small number of categories:

  • Clarification questions — "Can you explain what you mean by X?" (The reviewer did not fully understand something you said.)
  • Feasibility challenges — "How will you accomplish Y in only three weeks?" (The reviewer doubts the schedule or budget.)
  • Alternative solution questions — "Why did you choose this approach instead of Z?" (The reviewer wants to understand your decision-making.)
  • Risk probes — "What happens if the material you specified is not available?" (The reviewer is stress-testing your risk plan.)
  • Scope questions — "Is this too ambitious for one semester?" (The reviewer is concerned about project scale.)

Preparing for a defense means anticipating these categories and rehearsing answers. Walk through your proposal and ask yourself: If I were a skeptic, what would I challenge here? Write down your answers. Practice saying them aloud.

During the defense, if you do not know the answer to a question, say so directly: "That is a good point I had not fully considered — I would need to research X before I could answer that confidently." Honesty is more professional than a confident but incorrect answer.

docs.title: Proposal Review Criteria
docs.text:
A proposal review is only as useful as the criteria used to evaluate it. Proposal review criteria are the specific, measurable standards against which a proposal is judged. Clear criteria make the review fair and consistent, and they give the author actionable feedback rather than vague impressions.

In engineering practice, organizations develop standard review rubrics so that every proposal submitted to them is judged by the same bar. For this course, the review criteria cover five dimensions:

Dimension What Reviewers Evaluate Weight Problem Clarity Is the engineering need clearly defined with supporting evidence? 20% Solution Feasibility Is the proposed approach technically sound and achievable within constraints? 25% Planning Completeness Does the proposal include a realistic schedule, budget, and risk plan? 25% Communication Quality Is the proposal clearly written and the presentation professionally delivered? 20% Ethics and Safety Has the student addressed relevant ethical considerations and safety standards? 10%

These criteria align directly with the work you have done in Chapters 4, 5, and 6. A well-executed planning phase makes the proposal strong in the first three dimensions; the skills in this chapter address the fourth.

docs.title: Constructive Feedback
docs.text:
After every pitch, reviewers provide feedback to the presenter. The quality of that feedback depends on whether it is constructive — meaning it is specific, evidence-based, and actionable.

Constructive feedback is criticism that helps the recipient improve, as opposed to evaluative feedback that simply scores or judges. The difference is in what the recipient can do with it.

Compare these two examples:

  • Non-constructive: "Your budget section was weak."
  • Constructive: "Your budget lists a total cost of $85, but I could not find line items for the materials you mentioned in your methodology. Adding those line items would make the budget traceable and credible."

The second version identifies the specific gap, explains why it matters, and suggests exactly what to do. That is feedback the presenter can act on.

A widely used framework for structuring feedback is Situation-Behavior-Impact (SBI):

  • Situation — describe the specific context (which slide, which section, which moment in the presentation)
  • Behavior — describe what you observed, not what you inferred about the presenter's intention
  • Impact — describe how that observation affected you as a reviewer (it confused me, it convinced me, it left me with a question)

Applying SBI keeps feedback grounded in observable evidence rather than personal opinion, which makes it easier to receive and more professionally appropriate.

docs.title: The Peer Review Process
docs.text:
Peer review is the practice of having others who are working at a similar level evaluate each other's work using defined criteria. It is one of the most important quality-control mechanisms in engineering, science, and professional practice.

In this course, the peer review process gives you two different learning experiences simultaneously. When you review a classmate's proposal, you practice applying the evaluation criteria analytically — which reinforces your own understanding of what makes a proposal strong. When your proposal is reviewed by classmates, you receive feedback from multiple perspectives, which surfaces blind spots that a single reviewer (your teacher) might not catch.

The peer review process for the Project Proposal Pitch follows these steps:

  1. Presenter delivers the pitch (5–7 minutes)
  2. Reviewers take notes on the review rubric during the presentation
  3. Q&A / Defense (2–3 minutes)
  4. Reviewers complete their written rubric scores and comments (3 minutes, independently)
  5. Facilitator (teacher) synthesizes feedback and shares key themes with the presenter
  6. Presenter reflects and optionally responds to one piece of feedback they found most valuable

The written rubric is not optional. Oral-only feedback is hard to remember and easy to misinterpret. Written feedback creates a record the presenter can reference when revising their project plan.

docs.title: Diagram: Peer Review Rubric Simulator
docs.text:
Interactive Peer Review Rubric Simulator

Type: interactive infographic sim-id: peer-review-rubric Library: p5.js Status: Specified

Learning objective: Students will apply (Bloom L3) the five proposal review criteria by scoring a sample proposal video or transcript and generating a written feedback summary.

Canvas: 900 × 600 px, responsive.

Layout: Top section: A brief "sample proposal" text block (3–4 sentences summarizing a fictional student proposal) that the reviewer reads before scoring.

Middle section: Five criteria rows, each with: - Criterion name and one-sentence description - A 1–4 rating scale (1 = Does not meet standard, 2 = Approaches standard, 3 = Meets standard, 4 = Exceeds standard) implemented as clickable radio buttons - A text input field labeled "Specific evidence" where the reviewer types what they observed

Bottom section: - An auto-generated score bar (sum of five ratings, max 20 points) color-coded: 16–20 green, 11–15 yellow, below 11 red - A "Generate Feedback Summary" button that compiles the five evidence entries into a formatted paragraph using the SBI structure template - The generated paragraph appears below the button, copyable by clicking

Implementation: Embed as docs/sims/peer-review-rubric/index.html using p5.js CDN. Iframe at 660 px height.

docs.title: Being a Good Reviewee
docs.text:
The peer review process asks as much of the presenter as it does of the reviewer. Receiving critical feedback professionally — especially in a group setting — is a skill that takes practice.

Three habits of effective reviewees:

  1. Listen to understand, not to rebut. Your first instinct when hearing criticism may be to explain why the reviewer is wrong. Resist it. Take notes. Ask one clarifying question if needed: "When you say the budget was unclear, was it the line items or the totals that confused you?" Then thank the reviewer.

  2. Separate the work from yourself. A critique of your proposal is not a critique of your intelligence or effort. Reviewers who tell you "the risk section needs more depth" are giving you a gift — they are helping you strengthen your project before execution begins.

  3. Act on at least one piece of feedback. After the review session, identify the single most impactful piece of feedback you received and make a specific change to your project plan. This is what transforms peer review from an exercise into a genuine improvement mechanism.

docs.title: Chapter Summary
docs.text:
The proposal pitch is the capstone of your planning phase — the moment all of your prior work becomes a coherent argument for your project. Here is what you should be able to do after completing this chapter:

  • Write a complete engineering proposal with all standard sections, including a last-written executive summary
  • Structure slide presentations using the one-idea-per-slide, visuals-over-bullets, and contrast-hierarchy principles
  • Deliver a presentation at the appropriate pace with eye contact, clear voice projection, and confident posture
  • Anticipate and prepare for common defense question categories — clarification, feasibility, alternatives, risk, and scope
  • Apply the five review criteria (problem clarity, solution feasibility, planning completeness, communication quality, ethics and safety) to evaluate a peer's proposal
  • Write constructive feedback using the Situation-Behavior-Impact framework
  • Participate professionally in the peer review process as both presenter and reviewer

Your proposal pitch is also a rehearsal for the final presentation in Chapter 8. The habits you build here — particularly handling questions and receiving feedback — will serve you at the end of the course and throughout your engineering career.

docs.title: Chapter 8 - Project Execution, Checkpoints, and Final Presentation
Chapter 8: Project Execution, Checkpoints, and Final Presentation
Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers the full project execution phase — from building and testing a prototype, through modeling and simulation checkpoints, to documenting progress in a technical report and delivering a final presentation to stakeholders. Students apply systems thinking to their completed solution and demonstrate the technical and professional readiness they have developed across the entire course.

• Weekly Work Log — CTE-57, CTE-66, CTE-60
  • Checkpoint 1: Design Model Review — CTE-61
